An introduction to writing of fiction, poetry, and creative nonfiction. 
(PR: Completion of Core II composition requirement)

title: Creative Writing Poetry
description: Development of techniques and strategies for writing poetry in a variety of styles and forms.

title: Creative Writing Fiction
description: Development of techniques and strategies for writing fiction in a variety of styles and forms.

title: Creative Writing: Nonfiction
description: Development of techniques and strategies for writing creative nonfiction in a variety of styles and forms.

title: Comp & Rhet Preservice Teacher
description: Study of rhetorical invention and models of the composing process, with intensive practice in writing.

title: History English Language
description: The phonology, spelling, grammar, syntax, and vocabulary of previous language periods as background to Modern English.

title: Writing, Editing & Doc Design
description: Study of the principles and practices of professional writing, editing, and document design.

title: Writing in the Digital World
description: Development of writing skills and strategies with an emphasis on digital texts and genres.  (PR: Completion of Core II composition requirement)

title: Milton
description: Biographical and critical study, including Milton's English poetry and prose, and his literary and intellectual milieu.  (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Shaks Comedy Tragedy & Rom
description: Intensive study of Shakespeare's comedies, tragicomedies, and late romances.  Also includes the Sonnets and Venus and Adonis.  (PR: Any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Chaucer
description: The poetry of Chaucer, chiefly the Canterbury Tales, in the light of medieval tradition and critical analysis.  (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Shaks Hist & Tragedies
description: Intensive study of Shakespeare's histories and tragedies.  (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: 19th C British Novel
description: Austen, Scott, the Brontes, Gaskell, Dickens, Hardy, Schreiner, and others.

title: Victorian Poetry
description: Tennyson, Browning, Arnold, and others.(PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Victorian Nonfiction
description: Essays, speeches, treatises, and other works from Britain's Victorian age.  Includes such authors as Arnold, Carlyle, Darwin, Huxley, Eliot, Martineau, Mill, Newman, and others.(PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Addiction Literature
description: Study of literature that explores addiction as a primary theme.  Genres may include memoir and autobiography, fiction, poetry, graphic narratives, video, and digital-born texts. (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Approaches Teach Lit
description: The intensive study of the pedagogy of literature and literary critical theory and its classroom applications.

title: American Lit to 1830
description: Study of American literature of the Puritan, Colonial, and Federal periods, including such authors as Jonathan Edwards, Edward Taylor, Benjamin Franklin, Phillis Wheatley, Anne Bradstreet, Washington Irving, and James Fenimore Cooper. (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: American Lit 1830-1865
description: American literature of the Romantic period, including such authors as Emerson, Douglass, Poe, Melville, Hawthorne, Whitman, Dickinson, and lesser figures of the period. (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: American Lit 1865-1914
description: American literature of the Realistic and Naturalistic periods, including such authors as Howells, Crane, Twain, James, Chopin, Dreiser, Chesnutt,and Wharton. (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: American Literature after 1914
description: American literature after 1914, including such authors as Faulkner, Hemingway, Cather, Carver, Vonnegut, Morrison, and others. (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Appalachian Lit & Theory
description:
Appalachian Literature and Theory conducts an in-depth study of aspects of
Appalachian culture and literature through the lens of select literary and
social theories such as multiculturalism, feminism, or post-colonialism.

title: Studies in Authors or Genre
description: Intensive study focused on author(s) or genre. Topics will vary. May be repeated up to 6 hrs. with permission of chair.

title: International Literature
description: Readings in contemporary literature from the non-Anglo-European world. Texts will be taken from Asian, African, South American, Australian, and other authors. (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Multicultural Identities (MC)
description: Intensive exploration of multicultural identities in texts.  Topics may vary.  May be repeated up to 6 hrs. with permission of chair. (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Young Adult Literature
description: Examines literature written for adolescents and young adults, with a focus on coming of age and the formation of identity. (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Writing for Non-Profits
description: This foundational course will explore the goals and strategies specific to writing for non-profit organizations.  Students will read, analyze, and practice the real world writing tasks that non-profits require. (Completion of Core II Composition Requirement)

title: Contemporary Literature
description: Examines literature of the present, its influences, and the increasing diversification of cultural/textual production, including cinema, video, comix/manga, videogames, and blogs.  (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: 20th C Brit & Irish Poetry
description: Principal poetry since the Victorian period.   (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: 20th C American Poetry
description: Principal poetry since 1900.  (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Modernism
description: Examines literary modernism and the artistic, cultural, and historical contexts of that movement.  (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Medieval British Lit
description: Old English elegiac and heroic poetry; Middle English lyrics and romances; the Ricardian poets and Malory. (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Professional Writing Theory
description: Study of the major questions and theories relevant to professional writing.

title: Selected Topics in Film
description: Intensive study of a specific theme or genre.  Topics will vary.  May be repeated up to 6 hrs. with permission of chair.

title: Gender and Sexuality in Film
description: Exploration of evolving portrayals of gender roles and sexualities throughout film history and across film genres.

title: Screenwriting
description: Study of writing for film, including practice in methods to discover, organize, and draft screenplays for film production, with an emphasis on workshop methods for revision.

title: British Romantic Poets
description: Emphasis on Blake, Wordsworth, Coleridge, Byron, Shelley, and Keats.  (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: World Lit to Renaissance
description: Major works (excluding English), with emphasis on Homer, the Greek Drama, Virgil, Dante, and Cervantes.(PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: World Lit Since Renaissance
description: Major works (excluding English and American), with emphasis on Racine, Moliere, Goethe, and principal continental fiction.  (PR: any 200-level or 300-level ENG course EXCEPT 200, 204, 205, 314, or 354; or permission of chair)

title: Advanced Critical Theory
description: Intensive study of specific theorists, theoretical schools, or history of literary theory, including application to texts.  Topics will vary.  May be repeated up to 6 hrs. with permission of chair.

title: Comp Writing Center Theory
description: Introduces students to the study of teaching writing in a classroom setting and in one-to-one tutoring.

title: Literacy Studies
description: Theories of writing and reading development with a focus on cultural, linguistic, and rhetorical influences on literacy acquisition. (Completion of Core II Composition Requirement)

title: Visual Rhetoric
description: Study of the production, strategies, reception, and persuasive effects of visual texts. (Completion of Core II Composition Requirement)

title: Teaching Creative Writing
description: Pedagogy and practice for students who plan to teach Creative Writing at the primary, secondary, or college level.

title: Form/Theory: Creative Writing
description: Readings in creative writing illustrating formal and theoretical concerns.  Readings will also be applied to student writing.

title: Intro to Linguistics
description: The structural and descriptive approach to study of the English language. (Completion of Core II Composition Requirement)

title: Structures of English Language
description: Study of the structures of English grammar, including how these structures relate to punctuation, language acquisition, dialect variation, and the history of English. (Completion of Core II Composition Requirement)

title: Intro to Sociolinguistics
description: Sociolinguistics is the study of the effects of language in society, relevant to discourse practices, language attitudes variations, shifts, and changes. (Completion of Core II Composition Requirement)

title: Multi-Genre Creat Writ Wkshp
description: An advanced writing workshop to develop and refine creative work in a variety of genres, including poetry, fiction, and creative nonfiction.

title: Internship
description: A supervised internship.  The student works for a local firm/agency to gain practical experience in the major. Arranged by student and department.  Supervised by firm.

title: Poetry Workshop
description: A practical and intensive class in exploring the varieties of creative expression; exercises on the creating of verse in different forms and styles.

title: Fiction Workshop
description: Offers students a forum for presentation, discussion, and refinement of their work, either short stories or novels.

title: Creative Nonfiction
description: A writing workshop where students develop and refine their original creative nonfiction (memoir, biography, essays, travel/leisure writing, etc.), employing techniques typically reserved for fiction (dialogue, narrative, poetic language, etc.).

title: Senior Capstone
description:
An intensive examination of topics relevant to advanced English studies.  Provides capstone experience through substantial scholarly, creative, and/or pedagogical composition and presentation. (PR: ENG 350 , ENG 355 , and 12 hours of 400-level
English coursework at C or better; or permission of chair)

title: Smith StartUp Incubator Honors
description: An interdisciplinary experiential course that guides students entrepreneurs on discovering a problem they are passionate about solving and applying design thinking principles to solve it for one person.

title: Creativity & Innovation
description: Introduction to creativity theory, including exposure to basic frameworks, concepts, and obstacles to creativity.  Through practical application, the relationships between creativity, innovation and entrepreneurship are explored.

title: Intro to Entrepreneurship
description: A study of the functions, techniques and problems of management of the small business enterprise as opposed to the large-scale corporate situation.  The day section, in cooperation with the U.S. Small Business Administration, provides students with field experience as management consultants to an area small business.

title: Special Topics
description: Special topics in entrepreneurship.

title: Smith Incubator Honors II
description: An interdisciplinary experiential course that guides student start-ups on applying design thinking to innovate a solution that delights.

title: Marketing for Entrepreneurs
description: Survey of inbound digital marketing strategies that are cost effective for small firms and start-ups.  Strategies include search engine optimization and content marketing.

title: Business Models for Start-Ups
description: Identification and evaluation of business opportunities; Systematic analysis and design of critical start-up business components.

title: The Startup Experience
description: An experiential learning course, students will complete a portion of this course as an apprentice at a new or small business followed by experience starting an online business.

title: Dilemmas & Debates in Entrp
description: Refered by a faculty member, students will engage with entrepreneurs to discuss issues relevant to new ventures including partnerships, working with family, debt, ethical dilemmas, and customer issues.

title: Social Entrepreneurship
description: Introduces students to social entrepreneurship, social venture models, and funding options including philanthropy, government funding, and income generating, self-sustaining business models.

title: Corporate Intrapreneurship
description: Application of entrepreneurial mindset, behavior, and capability to the development of new opportunities within an existing organization.

title: Strategic Entrepreneurship
description: Lean startup and strategic thinking from both causation and effectuation viewpoints utilizing interdisciplinary skills to develop an original business concept, model, and plan.  Capstone course.

title: New Venture Launch
description: Working with advisor and mentors, students start their own ventures, develop prototypes (or service simulation), conduct market test, pivot on their original plans, and pitch ideas to local entrepreneur community.

title: Social Enterprise Practicum
description: Supervised field experience in a social enterprise for a minimum of 200 clock hours.  Regular conferences with instructor and weekly progress reports.

title: New Venture Practicum
description: Supervised field experience in a new or existing venture for a minimum of 200 clock hours.  Regular conferences with instructor and weekly progress reports.

title: Special Topics
description: Study of an advanced topic not normally covered in other courses.

title: Internship
description: A supervised environment working for a business firm/agency to gain practical experience by completing defined projects or tasks.

title: Env Assess Aqua Sys
description: Use of scientific procedures and current technology to characterize and quantify sensitive elements of aquatic ecosystems and to assess human impact on these systems.

title: Impact Assmt Documentation
description: Use of current concepts in conservation biology to examine the impacts of habitat perturbations on people and species of concern.

title: Independent Study
description: An approved study of special interest concerning environmental science, under the supervision of a faculty member. (PR: Permission of Instructor).

title: Independent Study
description: An approved study of special interest concerning environmental science, under the supervision of a faculty member. (PR: Permission of Instructor).

title: Independent Study
description: An approved study of special interest concerning environmental science, under the supervision of a faculty member. (PR: Permission of Instructor).

title: Independent Study
description: An approved study of special interest concerning environmental science, under the supervision of a faculty member. (PR: Permission of Instructor).

title: ES Capstone Project
description: An individualized environmental science capstone project, which will be planned and carried out under the supervision of a faculty member.  (Permission of Instructor and minimum of Junior standing).

title: Physiology of Fitness
description: The student will gain knowledge of behavior change, components of exercise sessions, cardinal principles of conditioning, basics of fitness programming, and instructing individual and group exercise sessions.

title: Intro to Exercise Science
description: Introductory overview of the current trends and theories in exercise science.  Preview the body systems, exercise training, and preparation for professional certification and careers in exercise science.

title: Fitness and Wellness
description: Course addresses fitness and weight control and modes of change.  Primary focus is on goals in nutrition, label identification, nutrient assessment, weight control, and fitness and how to reach them safely.

title: Phil Sport & Phys Activity
description: Development of philosophic reasoning skills to better understand the role that philosophy plays in our understanding and conception of physical activity and sport.

title: Exercise Physiology
description: Focuses on physiological and functional alterations in response to acute and chronic exercise with emphasis on metabolic, neuroendocrine, neuromuscular, cardiopulmonary, and environmental adaptations.

title: Exercise Physiology Lab
description: Laboratory course that demonstrates and reinforces concepts pertaining to the effects of exercise on physiological systems.

title: Fitness Assess & Exerc Prescr
description: Focuses on the processes and procedures of physical fitness evaluation and prescription. Emphasis is placed on the design of individual and group exercise programs.

title: Adult Fitness
description: Discuss health risk appraisal and exercise benefits of commonly seen pathophysiology among the U.S. adults.

title: Ancient & Medi Sport History
description: An in-depth investigation of the role of sport in ancient and medieval societies. The course focuses on ancient Greece, Rome, and medieval Europe, but will also briefly cover non-Western cultures.

title: Princ of Strength & Cond
description: Application of strength and conditioning principles in the development and administration of sport-based exercise programs. Emphasis on the teaching of flexibility, powerlifting, Olympic weightlifting, and speed and agility programs.

title: Prin of Strength Cond Lab
description: Laboratory course that demonstrates strength and conditioning skills. Practical application of strength and conditioning principles with emphasis on the teaching of flexibility, powerlifting, Olympic weightlifting, and speed and agility programs.

title: Advanced Exercise Physiology
description: The course in Advanced Exercise Physiology will focus on the detailed study of the cardiovascular, endocrine, immune, neuromuscular, and respiratory systems in response to acute and chronic exercise.

title: Drug and Sport
description: Examines the physiological mechanisms behind supplements and drugs used for exercise and athletic performance.

title: Internship Exercise Science
description: Field internship experience.

title: Strength & Condit Clin Fld Exp
description: The application and development of Strength and Conditioning Specialist skills in real-world settings.

title: Personal Finance (CT)
description: To assist the consumer in management of personal financial affairs.  Topics are consumerism, insurance, savings instruments, banking, and personal expenditures and budgeting, personal taxes, house buying, introduction to investments, and estate planning.

title: Risk Insurance Principles
description: Fundamental concepts and principles of risk; techniques used to manage pure risks, and the role of insurance and the insurance mechanism in handling the exposures of individuals and businesses.

title: Principles of Finance
description: Business finance from viewpoint of business manager; use of financial statements, tools, and concepts for measuring and planning for profitability and liquidity.

title: Life & Health Insurance
description: Legal facets of life, health, and annuity contracts; risk selection; programming; mathematics of life insurance; taxation; regulation of companies.

title: Prop & Liability Insur
description: Risk management and insurance tools applied to the needs of the corporate enterprise; direct/indirect property exposures; third-party claims; workers compensation, fidelity; crime; boiler/machinery; valuation and insurance surveys.

title: Intermediate Financial Manage
description: Application of financial principles to corporate business problems.  Computer analysis will be utilized where appropriate.

title: Finan Mgt Health Care Org
description: Management of working capital, evaluation of financial data, capital budgeting, the capitalism process, and the study of third party reimbursement systems.

title: Commercial Banking
description: Bank structure; asset and liability management; management of reserves; liquidity management; credit analysis and loan administration; costs and pricing of bank services; analysis of bank performance and capital adequacy; evolution of the "financial supermarket."

title: Principles of Investment
description: A study of financial market operations, security analysis and portfolio selection.  Models of capital market equilibrium, trade-off between risk and return, and how to evaluate portfolio performance are also discussed.

title: Entrepreneurial Finance
description:
Entrepreneurial finance examines the principles of small business finance which include projecting
financial needs and surveying potential sources of financing. Other areas covered include
financial  forecasting, and sources of capital.

title: Social Ins Employee Benefits
description: Coverage and limitations of social insurance; social security; worker's compensation; unemployment insurance; medicare, medicaid, integration with private insurance and employee benefits; theory of group programs; pension plans.

title: Corporate Risk Management
description: Identification, analysis, and handling of the risk exposures faced by businesses and risk managers; loss prevention and control; risk retention; self-insurance and corporate insurance programs.

title: Portfolio Analysis and Manage
description: Analytical procedures for valuing various financial securities and techniques for the creation and maintenance of portfolios.

title: Futures and Options
description: To introduce options and futures, their market microstructure, their theoretical foundation pertaining to pricing and hedging with such contracts, and their uses.

title: Financial Advising: FINRA S-7
description: This course prepares students for the Series 7 exam that entitles the holder to sell all types of securities products with the exception of commondities and futures.

title: International Fin Management
description: International financing techniques and the role of finance in multinational organizations.

title: Financial Policies/Strategies
description: Financial planning, working capital management, capital budgeting, dividend policy and comprehensive problems. Capstone Experience.

title: Intl Business Strategies
description: Strategies for gaining competitive advantage in the global business environment. Topics include international trade and investment, economic growth, and operations of multinational corporations. Capstone Experience for International Business major.

title: Special Topics
description: Study of an advanced topic not normally covered in other courses.  Finance majors only, with permission of department chair.

title: Internship
description: A supervised internship in which the student works for a business firm/agency to gain practical experience in the student's major.  The program of work and study will be defined in advance and the students performance will be evaluated.

title: First Yr Sem Critical Thinking
description: Students will learn critical thinking skills integral to life-long learning through discussion, interaction, discovery, problem-solving, writing, research, reflection, and examination of multicultural/international issues.

title: First Year Seminar-Honors
description: Students will develop intentional critical thinking skills integral to lifelong learning through inquiry, discussion, interaction, discovery, problem-solving, writing, research, reflection and examination of complex multicultural/global ideas and themes.

title: Intro to Forensic Science
description: The relationship between scientific process and crime solutions is explored.  DNA technology, probability theory, fingerprints, blood spatter, questioned documents, crime scene investigation will be examined using laboratories and case studies.

title: Elementary French I
description: Pronunciation, conversation, reading, and composition with emphasis on aural/oral development.

title: Elementary French II
description: Pronunciation, conversation, reading, and composition with emphasis on aural/oral development.

title: Intermediate French III
description: Continuation on the intermediate level of the basic language skills: pronunciation, conversation, reading, and composition with emphasis on aural/oral development.

title: Intermediate French IV
description: Development of practical conversational skills, reading for comprehension, and directed composition.

title: French Society & Life
description: Selected topics relating to culture and life in the Frenchspeaking countries.  Lectures, readings, and discussions in English.

title: Masterpieces in Trans
description: This course deals with major works of French literature and requires no prior background in French literature history. The course will be conducted in English.

title: Intro to FRN Comp & Conv
description: Writing/Speaking intensive course designed to develop communicative skills and review language fundamentals acquired in FRN 101 204 sequence (2 years). Course taught in French. PR: FRN 204 )

title: Adv Grammar & Composition
description: Study of idioms, grammatical structure, and syntax with emphasis on free composition, use of language laboratory, and formal study of the art of translation from English to French.

title: Survey French Literature
description: A study of important literary movements, representative authors and their works from the Middle Ages to present.

title: Adv Frn Gram & Oral Com
description: Analysis of grammatical structures and introduction to phonetics. Oral and written exercises, presentations, and discussion. Course conducted mainly in French. (PR: FRN 204 )

title: 17th Century French Theater
description: Study of representative plays by the classical dramatists Corneille, Moliere, and Racine.  This course is conducted entirely in French.

title: 18th Century French Lit
description: Study of representative works by the philosophes, Montesquieu, Rousseau, Voltaire, and Diderot.  The course will be conducted in French.  (PR: FRN 204 )

title: 19th Century French Novel
description: A study of major novels chosen to illustrate the romantic, realistic, and naturalistic literary movements.  The course is conducted in French.

title: 20th Century French Novel
description: A study of representative 20th Century French novels by Proust, Malraux, Sartre, Camus, Robbe-Grillet.  The course will be taught in French.

title: Contemporary Frn Film
description:
Course on selected films by French directors of the New Wave and 
the Post New-Wave period.

title: Devel of French Prose I
description: An analysis of the important works that mark the evolution of French Prose writers from the Middle Ages through the 18th Century.  Readings and discussions are in French.

title: Dev Frn Poetry-Theater I
description: A study of literary works illustrating the development of poetry and theater in France from the Middle Ages through the 18th Century.

title: Special Topics
description: A course for advanced students sufficiently prepared to do constructive work in phases of the language or literature of interest to them.

title: Special Topics
description: A course for advanced students sufficiently prepared to do constructive work in phases of the language or literature of interest to them.

title: Intro to Human Geography (CT)
description: This critical thinking course provides a systematic examination of contemporary concepts and processes of human geography in global perspective, including economics, geopolitics, culture, nationalism, urbanization, governance, agriculture, population, and migration.

title: Physical Geography (CT)
description: Study of Earth's natural systems, including the atmosphere, landforms/soils, ecosystems/biomes, hazards, and the hydrosphere, and the interactions between them and human activities using hands-on labs, geospatial technologies, and fieldwork.

title: Physical Geography Lab
description:
Lab to accompany Physical Geography lecture exploring earth-sun
relationships, maps, atmosphere, climate, weather, hydrologic cycle,
the biosphere, soils, landforms, erosion, vulcanism, and rivers.

title: Basic GIS
description:
Introduction to GIS concepts including GIS components, spatial and tabular data, database
elements, data formats, and map design; hands-on experience with a GIS.

title: Air Photos & Satellite Imagery
description: Introduction to photogrammetry and remote sensing through the hands-on investigation of aerial photographs and satellite imagery using the latest technology.

title: Smartphone GPS
description: Principles of Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS); using your smartphone for Global Positioning Systems (GPS)/GNSS orientation, and data collection.

title: Web GIS
description: Students learn to build web maps, share GIS data on the internet, and create web GIS applications.

title: Economic Geography (CT)
description: A systematic examination of world economic geography with a focus on population, agriculture, transportation, land use, urbanization, industry, energy, and the environment.

title: Geography West Virginia
description: A survey of the geography of West Virginia including landforms, climate, settlement patterns, population, economics, resources, politics, and environmental changes.

title: Biblical Geography
description: Students explore the physical and human geography of ancient Palestine and assess how geography informs our understanding of people, events, and places described by Jewish and Christian scriptures.

title: Global Environment Issues (CT)
description: Environmental issues have great emotional, political, and economic significance. The dynamics of global environmental problems, their complex interactions, and effects on potential stakeholders will be examined at the international scale.

title: Mapping Our World
description: Students use GIS, GPS, Remote Sensing, and Drones for map data collection, map creation, map analysis, and to investigate how these tools function for studying human and physical Earth systems.

title: Physical Meteorology (CT)
description: Explore how the atmosphere works and why weather happens, focusing on energy, clouds, precipitation, winds, and analysis of weather maps, radar, and satellite data.

title: Physical Meteorology Lab
description: Hands-on laboratory emphasizing measurement of atmospheric conditions using standard meteorological instruments.

title: Remote Pilot Ground School
description: This course provides the fundamental knowledge to take the FAA Part 107 Remote Pilot exam and how to safely operate as a remote pilot-in-command (RPIC).

title: Methods in Geography
description: Provides an introduction to research methods in geography.  Topics addressed include: data sources; sampling; data collection, storage, and management; descriptive measures and data visualization techniques; qualitative and quantitative analysis.

title: Geog of USA & Canada
description: Survey of physical, historical, population, economic, political, cultural, and regional geographies of Canada and the United States, including an analysis of the multi-cultural nature of the societies.

title: World Regional Geography
description: World regions examined using a synthesis of physical and human geographical themes including environment, culture, landscape, climate, landforms, globalization, population patterns, urbanization, economies, and political geography.

title: Geographic Information Systems
description: Geographic Information Systems (GIS) principles, techniques, and applications for the social and natural sciences, emphasizing core geographic concepts through an integrated lecture and lab format.

title: Aviation Weather
description: A study of atmospheric conditions on aeronautical operations.  Topics include atmospheric properties, aviation hazards including thunderstorms, wind shear, turbulence, and icing, and weather flight resources.

title: Geography of Appalachia
description: A study of the geography of Appalachia, including landforms, climate, settlement patterns, population, economics, resources, politics, and environmental changes.

title: Geography of Europe
description: An examination of the geography of Europe focusing on contemporary issues, including climate, culture, economics, environmental change, everyday life, international relations landforms, language, politics, population, religion, and urbanization.

title: Political Geography
description:
A systematic and regional survey of world political problems and
international relations stressing current geopolitical conflicts.

title: Population Geography
description: An examination of contemporary patterns, processes, and problems of population geography in global perspective, including fertility, mortality, demographic change, migration, malnutrition, urbanization, natural resource sustainability, and future projections.

title: Geog of South & Middle America
description:
A study of settlement, transportation, manufacturing, agriculture,
geopolitics, and natural resources of South and Middle American countries.

title: Urban Geography
description: Study of the evolution, morphology and land use, functions, and problems of urban areas, with emphasis on governance, planning, and the social and environmental impacts of urbanization.

title: Medical Geography
description: An examination of contemporary issues and problems in health and medical geography, including the spatial aspects of global health, health care policy, and disease origins, diffusion, and ecology.

title: Principles Methods of Planning
description: An examination of contemporary planning focusing on principles, methods, techniques, and tools; and the political, legal, and ethical contexts of planning.

title: Geography For Educators
description: A study of geographic concepts, methods, and technologies essential for effective teaching of geographic content for K-12 social studies teachers and college educators.

title: Cartography & GIS
description: This course is an introduction to cartography and is the cornerstone of Geographic Information Science.  In this class you will learn basic map design, map interpretation and appreciation.  We will explore the nature of spatial data, and learn what maps can and cannot represent.  We will study maps as a data source for Geographic Information Systems, and as a graphic tool for scientific visualizations.

title: Transportation Geography
description: A geographic analysis of transportation and its spatial organization. Concepts, models, and analytical methods related to traffic demand, network configuration, and allocation of transport facilities are covered.

title: Climatology
description: A study of elements of weather and climate, methods of climatic classification, and the distribution and characteristics of world climate regions.

title: Principles of GIS
description: Introduction to Geographic Information Systems (GIS) principles, techniques, and applications for the social and natural sciences with emphasis on foundational geographic principles in a lecture/lab format.

title: Programming GIS
description: Students automate GIS analyses and workflows by building repeatable models, programming with Python, and using other concepts and techniques within both desktop and web GIS applications.

title: GIS Location Analytics
description: This course explores how Geographic Information Systems (GIS) are used in business for market analysis, site selection, logistics, and data-driven decision-making.

title: Environmental Raster GIS
description: Environmental Applications of GIS raster analysis, including local, neighborhood, and zonal operations, terrain analysis, distance modeling, and surface interpolation, data collection and input, and advanced spatial analysis using GIS.

title: Remote Sensing & Photogram
description: Scientific study of the earth using images and data captured using satellite- or aircraft-borne sensors, with emphasis on issues of acquisition, photogrammetric interpretation, spatial analysis and application.

title: Floods Hazards and GIS
description: Application of principles of flood hazards preparation, disaster management, and mitigation using Geographic Information Systems (GIS).

title: Spatial Statistics using GIS
description: This course equips students with the skills to understand, apply, and critically evaluate geostatistical methods in GIS, emphasizing spatial data analysis, model development, visualization, and real-world problem-solving.

title: Extreme Weather & Mesoscale
description: Study of mesoscale and extreme weather systems, including thunderstorms, squall lines, tornadoes, and winter storms. Emphasizes radar, satellite, and model interpretation for mesoscale weather analysis and forecasting.

title: Digital Meteorology
description: This course equips students with the skills to retrieve and analyze weather data, curate information for public dissemination, and create engaging audio-visual content.

title: Drones: Remote Sensing & GIS
description: Learn FAA rules and safety procedures; prepare for Remote Pilot licensing exam; operate drones to collect remote sensing data; process imagery for analysis; integrate sUAS imagery with existing GIS data.

title: Weather Analysis
description: Introduction to reading weather maps and meteorological analysis techniques including satellite and radar image interpretation and numerical weather prediction.

title: Internship
description: Cooperative learning  experience with regional employer/government agency.

title: Senior Capstone
description:
This senior capstone course combines research methods, data collection, and analysis to produce original research on questions and problems in geography.
Par I: Pilot Project.

title: Senior Capstone II
description: This senior capstone course combines research methods, data collection, and analysis to produce original research on questions and problems in geography. Part II.

title: Earth's Fury and Fortune
description: Introductory course for non-science/ science majors on societal impacts and mitigation of volcanic, earthquake, tsunami, landslide, flood, and climate change hazards, and origin and extraction of earth and energy resources.

title: Intro Oceanography
description: Origin of the seas and ocean basins.  Processes of marine sedimentation and seawater chemistry.  Dynamics of air/sea interaction, circulation, waves and tides.  Description of coastal and other marine environments.

title: Intro Oceanography Lab
description: A complementary laboratory to Introductory Oceanography, GLY 150 .  A series of exercises relating to bathymetry, accoustic profiling, marine charts, properties of seawater, sea floor sediments, currents, waves and tides.

title: Geology of the National Parks
description: An introduction to U.S. Parks and Monuments including earth materials, plate tectonic setting, formative geologic processes, and natural history; brief overview of history and purpose of the National Park Service.

title: The Dynamic Earth
description: An elementary but comprehensive physical geology course that deals with the earth's origin, composition, structures, tectonics and processes.  Intended primarily for, but not limited to, the science major.  3 lec.

title: The Earth Through Time
description: Introduction to 1) the principles used to interpret past physical, biologic, and chemical events archived in rock record, and 2)the history and development of the earth's lithosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere.

title: Earth Materials Lab
description: An introduction to laboratory methods for the identification and classification of minerals, rocks, fossils and landscapes.

title: Earth Through Time Lab
description: Reconstruction of events in earth history based on physical characteristics and arrangement of rock layers and their fossil contens. 2 hr lab (PR: 210L; CR: GLY 201 )

title: Geologic Field Methods
description: Using the geology of West Virginia, introduces geologic field methods including geologic map interpretation and construction, cross-section preparation, qualitative and quantitative data collection, and ArcGIS mapping. Includes fields trips, laboratories.

title: Structural Geology
description: Explores geologic structures: their history, 3-d relationships and the stresses that cause them. Structural deformation occurs at scales ranging form the continental to the atomic. Labs and weekend field trip.

title: Mineralogy
description: Identification, classification, orgin, occurrences, and economic uses of minerals and their crystallographic forms. 3 lec-2 lab.

title: Geology Lab Techniques
description: Techniques of collection, preparation and analysis of mineral, rock and water samples, and the use of different instruments for obtaining quality data. Will also cover tools used for data interpretation.

title: Stratigraphy & Sediment
description: Formation, organization, sequence, and correlation of sedimentary rocks; study of the orgin, transportation and deposition of rock-forming sediments. 3 lec-2 lab.

title: Tectonics
description: Overview of tectonics theory and the processes at plate boundaries that form orogenic systems, continents and ocean basins. Students will read, discuss and present scientific literature.

title: Big Bend Field Excursion
description: Field trip to Big Bend National Park, Texas to study the structure, stratigraphy, igneous geology, metamorphic geology, paleontology and natural history of this national park.

title: Invertebrate Paleontology
description: Taxonomy, morphology, and paleoecology of body and trace fossils representing the major invertebrate phyla; analysis and interpretation of faunal assemblages; evolution and extinction of species. (PR: GLY 201 )

title: Principles of Geochemistry
description: Application of chemical principles to geology.  Topics include cosmochemistry; distribution of elements in minerals and rocks; aqueous solutions and water-rock interaction; radiometric age dating and stable isotope geology.

title: Igneous& Metamorphic Petrology
description: Indentification and classification of igneous, and metamorphic rocks, their origin and occurrence; their geologic and economic importance. 3 lec-2 lab.

title: Sedimentary Petrology
description: Microscopic description of minerals, textures, and fossils  in sedimentary rocks, their classification, and interpretation of source area, depositional environment and post-depositional history. 3 lec-2 lab.

title: Applied Geophysics
description: Explore cutting-edge techniques to image Earth's hidden structures and dynamic processes. Apply seismic, gravity, magnetic, resistivity, and ground-penetrating radar methods to problems in environmental, engineering, archaeological, economic, and forensic geology.

title: Fossil Fuels
description: Origin and distribution of coal, oil and gas, and methods of exploration and reserve evaluaton. 3 lec-2 lab.

title: Geoarchaeology
description: Application of geological methods and principles to study of archaeological sites and contents. Focused on analysis of geological samples for interpretation of site matrix, site formation processes, and paleoenvironmental setting.

title: Principles Geomorphology
description: Principles of identification and analysis of the world's surficial features in terms of stratigraphy, structure, processes, tectonics and time. 3 lec. 2 lab.

title: Hydrogeology
description: The properties of water, the hydrologic cycle with emphasis on surface and groundwater processes, the uses, needs and problems associated with water resources. 3 lec.

title: Hydrogeology Laboratory
description: A two-hour laboratory of practical hydrogeologic problem solving. For non-majors, elective.

title: Environmental Geology
description: Consideration of risks posed by natural geo-hazards and from physical/chemical contamination of geologic media. (PR: GLY 200 , GLY 210L or equivalent; Recommended: GLY 451 )

title: Engineering Geology
description: Consideration of geotechnical problems faced by geologists and engineers. Major topics include mechanics and classification of soil and rock, and geotechnical aspects of groundwater.

title: Capstone Experience
description: An independent study involving a research project or internship.  Must be approved by Geology faculty.  (PR: 20 hours of Geology coursework).

title: Capstone Class
description: A seminar-type class on a multidisciplinary applied topic offered at the discretion of faculty to substitute for GLY 491 . (PR: 20 hours of Geology coursework / consent of faculty).

title: Elementary German I
description: Pronunciation, conversation, reading, and composition with an emphasis on aural/oral development.

title: Elementary German II
description: Pronunciation, conversation, reading, and composition with an emphasis on aural/oral development.

title: Intermediate German I
description: Continuation on the intermediate level of the basic language skills: pronunciation, conversation, reading, and composition with an emphasis on aural/oral development. (PR: GER 102 or 3 units of high school German)

title: Intermediate German II
description: Continuation on the intermediate level of the basic language skills: pronunciation, conversation, reading and composition with an emphasis on aural/oral development. (PR: GER 203 or 4 units of high school German)

title: German Society & Life
description: Study of selected topics relating to culture and life in the German speaking countries. Lectures, readings, and discussions in English, No prerequisite.

title: Advanced Grammar, Conv, Comp
description: Study of idioms, grammatical structure, and syntax through conversation and composition.

title: Advanced Grammar & Conv & Comp
description: Study of idioms, grammatical structure, and syntax through conversation and composition.

title: Grimm's Fairy Tales
description: Taught in English this course will focus on many of the Brothers Grimm's collection of 279 fairy tales, augmented by additional readings and film showings.

title: Intermediate Ancient Greek 1
description: Varied readings including selections form Homer's Iliad, Dialogues of Plato and the New Testament.

title: Intermediate Ancient Greek 3
description: Intermediate Ancient Greek familiarizes you with translating continuous original texts from the ancient world.

title: Advanced Ancient Greek Prose
description: A close reading of Ancient Greek prose authors

title: Advanced Ancient Greek Poetry
description: A close reading of Ancient Greek authors of poetry.

title: First Aid Child & Infants
description: First aid and CPR for children and infants related to the home, school, and playground.

title: Health Iss in PE and Ath
Intro to the Health Profession
description: This course is designed to provide an overview of the health care system and health professions taught within the MU campus and throughout the state.

title: Health Sciences Seminar
description: This course is designed to bring outside non-clinical speakers to discuss professionalism in health care.  Students analyze career opportunities and establish non-clinical career expectations.

title: Intro to Human Diseases in HS
description: A clinical overview of the common human diseases at a patient and scientist level, with emphasis on bridging the communication gap between health professionals, patients, and the general public.

title: Intro to Res in Health Prof
description: This course covers the basic quantitative and qualitative research techniques used in the health professions.

title: Allied Health Sciences Admin
description: In this course students will explore and apply allied health sciences principles as they related to the student's focus in the Health Sciences major.

title: BHS Senior Seminar
description: Opportunity for students to explore current topics related to areas of interest.

title: Special Topics in HP
description: Courses will be developed in special topics in Health Professions as deemed necessary by the Dean, COHP

title: Special Topics in HP
description: Courses will be developed in special topics in Health Professions as deemed necessary by the Dean, COHP

title: Special Topics in HP
description: Courses will be developed in special topics in Health Professions as deemed necessary by the Dean, COHP

title: Special Topics in HP
description: Courses will be developed in special topics in Health Professions as deemed necessary by the Dean, COHP

title: Capstone
description: This course offers an opportunity for students to demonstrate that they have achieved the goals for learning established the Bachelor of Health Sciences Program.

title: Comp Medical Terminology (CT)
description: This course is designed to introduce students to basic medical terminology and basic pharmacology.

title: Intro Appl Anat & Physiol
description: Focuses on basic anatomy and physiology as applied to physical activity.

title: Practical & Emerging Tech AT
description: This course introduces students to taping, wrapping, and advanced emergency care techniques used in athletic training. (PR: Admission to the Athletic Training Program)

title: Intro to Athletic Training
description: Survey and study of the basic techniques and practices of athletic training.

title: Personal Health
description: A survey course that focuses upon wellness promotion and prevention of various health problems.

title: Personal Health II
description: An examination of the health content areas of mental/emotional health, substance use/abuse, and human sexuality/family life education.

title: Hlth Prov First Aid/CPR/AED
description: First Aid, CPR, and AED skills for health care providers.  Additional topics include musculoskeletal injuries, environmental conditions, and sudden illness.

title: Orthopedic Skills
description: An intensive hands-on course for allied health majors that introduces the orthopedic assessment and testing skills needed to perform effective evaluations on the field and in the clinical setting.

title: Intro to Worksite Wellness
description: An introduction to worksite wellness programming including the basic components of an effective wellness program and how to integrate wellness into corporate culture.

title: AT Clinical Exp: Level I
description:
To begin developing athletic training evaluation and treatment skills under the direction of a BOC certified Athletic Trainer.  Requires 150 clinical hours.  
(CR: Admission to the Athletic Training Program)

title: Fund Video Analysis Mvmt (CT)
description: Fundamental concepts and techniques for analyzing human movement during sport, exercise, and occupation.  Students will develop qualitative and video-based analysis skills as used in sports science labs and rehabilitation clinics.

title: Worksite Health Assessment
description: An investigation into worksite health assessment models and modes.

title: Org & Admin Worksite Wellness
description: This is a course that investigates current trends in worksite wellness organization & administration and provides instruction of wellness and prevention interventions into corporate culture and business plans.

title: Worksite Wellness Prescription
description: This is a course that investigates prescription of worksite wellness programming based on data collection methods and current industry Best Practices for wellness interventions.

title: Eval Worksite Wellness Program
description: This course investigates the effectiveness of worksite wellness programming.  Students will understand what components of a program should be evaluated and how to calculate return on investment for intervention.

title: AT Clinical Exp: Level II
description: To continue developing athletic training evaulation and treatment skills under the direction of a BOC certified Athletic Trainer.  Requires 150 clinical hours.          (PR: HS255)

title: AT Clinical Exp: Level III
description: To continue developing athletic training evaluation and treatment skills under the direction of a BOC certified Athletic Trainer and/or other qualified allied health professionals.  Requires 150 clinical hours. (PR: HS360)

title: Functional Kinesiology
description: Applied anatomy of the human musculature and biomechanics in relation to physical activity.

title: Functional Kinesiology Lab
description: The analysis of human movement based on anatomical and mechanical principles. Emphasis is given to the application of these principles to the understanding of human movement and athletic performance.

title: Motor Learning
description: A study of the factors contributing to the acquisition, improvement and retention of gross motor skills. Stages of motor development and learning will be examined from a behavioral approach.

title: Applied Res Mthd Hlth Mvmt Sc
description: Applied Research Methods in Health & Movement Sciences is an introduction to research methods for medical/health fields such as athletic training, biomechanics, exercise science, strength & conditioning, and kinesiology.

title: Biomech Testing & Interpret
description: Experiential learning of the procedures and processes of human movement testing and reporting using biomechanical equipment.

title: Sport Psychology
description: An examination of theory, research, and application of psychological principles related to sport and exercise.

title: Organ Admin in Kines
description: This course investigates current trends in administration and organization in Kinesiology. Areas considered include but are not limited to policy planning, leadership, ethics, management and current organizational trends in Kinesiology.

title: Ortho Assess Upper Extremity
description: Orthopedic evaluation techniques of the thoracic and cervical spines and the upper extremities.  Anatomy, injury recognition, stress and special testing, treatment protocols, and preventative measure will be examined.

title: Ortho Assess Lower Extremity
description: Orthopedic evaluation techniques of the lumbar spine and the lower extremities.  Anatomy, injury recognition, stress and special testing, treatment protocols, and preventative measures will be examined.

title: Applied Program Movement Sci
description: Introduces programming concepts for analyzing movement-related data, enabling students to design reusable code and apply computational tools to research and practice in sport, exercise, rehabilitation, and health sciences.

title: Therapeutic Interventions I
description: Therapeutic modality application in the initial stages of injury, and modality and therapeutic exercise selection and prescription based upon patient responses for injuries to the lower extremity and lumbar spine.

title: Therapeutic Interventions II
description:
Instruction of electrical stimulation modalities and therapeutic exercise rehabilitation of the upper extremity and cervical spine, including post-surgical rehabilitation.  Strategies for psychosocial intervention will also be addressed.
(PR: HS 448 )

title: AT Clinical Exp: Level IV
description: To continue developing athletic training evaluation and treatment skills under the direction of a BOC certified Athletic Trainer and/or other qualified allied health professionals.  Requires 150 clinical hours. (PR: HS361)

title: Pathomechanics
description: An investigation into normal and abnormal human movement patterns in sport, the workplace, and in activities of daily living.

title: Biomechanical Analy of Mvmt
description:
An investigation into the science of human motion and its
relationship to the envirionment in which it is performed.

title: Applied Independent Res Technq
description: Applied Independent Research Techniques allows a student to complete a research project with a faculty research mentor as an undergraduate student.

title: Seminar in Sports Science
description: This course examines concepts related to acquiring, analyzing, and interpreting data relevant to human performance outcomes within sport, exercise, tactical operations and medical return to play.

title: Research Practicum Biomechanic
description: This course offers "hands-on" work within the biomechanics lab.  The student will assist with current research.  This experience that allows students to gain practical experience within a lab setting.

title: Internship
description:
Supervised clinical experience in an approved setting.  Can be repeated for a total of 8hrs.  Capstone experience requires 3hrs minimum in one term.
(PR: program permission)

title: Trends in Biomechanics II
description: A final investigation into normal and abnormal human movement patterns in sport, the workplace, and activities of daily living.

title: History Methods Lab
description: An introduction to the fundamental methods of the discipline.

title: Great Civs to 1300 (CT)
description: Comparative study of the origin and course of major civilizations focusing on the Middle East, India, China, and  the West. This class emphasizes critical thinking skills.

title: Great Civs to 1300 (CT)
description: Comparative study of the origin and course of major civilizations focusing on the Middle East, India, China, and the West.  This honors section emphasizes critical thinking skills.

title: World & West 1300-1850 (CT)
description: An interdisciplinary analysis of the foundations of Western development. This  class emphasizes critical thinking skills.

title: World & West 1300-1850 (CT)
description: An interdisciplinary analysis of the foundations of Western development.  This class emphasizes critical thinking skills.

title: The World Since 1850 (CT)
description: Major world developments and trends from the middle of the 19th century to the present and their implications for the future. This class emphasizes critical thinking skills.

title: World Since 1850 Honors (CT)
description: Survey for superior students of world developments and trends from the 19th century to the present and their implications for the future.

title: The Developing World (CT)
description: A survey of selected countries within the Developing World (Asia, Latin America, Africa), focusing on imperialism, colonialism and development theory. This class emphasizes critical thinking skills.

title: History in Action
description: This course will explore the history of Huntington through the methodology of public history.

title: Ancient Egypt
description: A survey of the history of Egypt from the pre-dynastic kings through the reign of Cleopatra with emphasis on religious, cultural, social, political and economic developments.

title: Ancient History
description: A survey of the ancient Near East, Greece, and Rome with emphasis on Greek and Roman civilization from Mycenaean times through the Roman Empire of the 5th century.  Open to all undergraduates.

title: European Hist Medieval
description: A survey of the history of Europe from the later Roman Empire to the end of the Middle Ages with emphasis on religious, cultural, social, political and economic developments.  Open to all undergraduates.

title: Rise & Fall Nazi Germany
description: A study of the origins, course, and collapse of the Third Reich.  Some attention will be given to pre-Nazi period.

title: American History to 1877 (CT)
description: A general survey from the discovery in 1492 through the period of Reconstruction. This class emphasizes critical thinking skills.

title: Amer Hist to 1877-Honors
description: A survey of American History to 1877 for the superior student.

title: American Hist From 1877 (CT)
description: A general survey since Reconstruction. This class emphasizes critical thinking skills.

title: Women in US History
description: A study of the public and private contributions of women in the shaping of the United States from the Colonial period to the present.

title: Rise of Islam 570-1750
description: A study of the Middle Eastern region from pre-Islamic Arabia to the pinnacle of Ottoman imperial control. Emphasis is placed on cultural, social, and political developments.

title: Modern East Asia
description: A survey of the history of East Asia from 17th century to present with a focus on China and Japan.

title: Special Topics
description: Selected topics not covered in regular course offerings.

title: Special Topics
description: Selected topics not covered in regular course offerings.

title: Special Topics
description: Selected topics not covered in regular course offerings.

title: Special Topics
description: Selected topics not covered in regular course offerings.

title: History Research Option
description: Guided historical research and writing.

title: Spanish History Since 1475
description: A survey of Spain's historical development emphasizing her rise and decline as a world power, the impact of persistent internal conflict, the Franco dictatorship and the transition to democracy.

title: Drug Wars
description:
This course examines the history of the war on drugs throughout U.S. and
Latin American history with an emphasis on the past century.

title: The Holocaust
description: An exploration of the why and how of the Holocaust with an emphasis on Nazi Jewish policy from 1933 to 1942.

title: The Global Cold War
description:
An international survey of World War II and the Cold War.  Topics include military,
political, diplomatic, social and cultural history from a global perspective.

title: Baseball in the Americas
description: It seeks to understand the importance of baseball from a social, cultural and political standpoint.  It also explores the use of baseball as a diplomatic tool.

title: History of the Cherokee
description: The course explores the history and culture of the Cherokee people from pre-contact through the 21st century.

title: History of Global Pandemics
description: A study of pandemics in world history, from the Plague of Athens to COVID-19.

title: World War I
description: This course explores the First World War.  It examines the roots of the conflict, the outbreak, the course of the war, and its chaotic aftermath.

title: African-American History
description: A survey of African-American History from African and West Indian origins to the present.

title: History of the British Empire
description: A historical survey of the global nature of the British Empire that lasted for more than 300 years and impacted every part of the world.

title: LGBTQ+ History of the US
description: A survey of LGBTQIA+ history in the United States from the colonial period to the present.

title: European Hist 1492-1815
description: A survey of European history emphasizing the Renaissance, the Reformation and the rise of the national states.

title: Football and American Culture
description: This class examines the role of football in American culture, politics, the economy and particularly its role in the elevation of college sports.

title: American Colonial History
description: A study of the historical development of the English colonies in America.

title: American Legal History
description: Historical development of American law in areas ranging from slavery and racial discrimination to civil liberties and crime and punishment.

title: The US in the 1970s
description: The economic, political, social and cultural history of the United States as it shifted from the modern 1960s to the post-modern world of the late 20th century.

title: The United States in the 1980s
description: Focusing on the United States in the 1980s global context, this course examines the social, cultural, political, and economic history of the late 20th century.

title: Sports and Civil Rights
description: An examination of the way that sports history reflects important themes in US history such as equality, race, ethnicity, and gender.

title: The Modern Middle East
description: A survey of the modern Middle East with emphasis on the historical background of current controversial issues confronting the region.

title: The Civil Rights Movement
description: An exploration of 20th century movements for civil rights in American history examining race, ethnicity, social class, and gender.

title: Conspiracies in US History
description: This course focuses on the ways people have constructed and used conspiracy theories to explain events in U.S. political, cultural, social, and scientific arenas.

title: China & West 1500-1900
description: Focuses on China's intellectual, political, diplomatic and social responses to the aggressive expansion of the modern West.

title: Asia to 1600
description: This course will introduce students to the political,  cultural, social, and intellectual environment of premodern Asia.  Students will also learn to think critically about their own and other societies.

title: Food in World History
description: This course examines the key changes and continuities in human food consumption patterns and practices since the Neolithic period while comparing and contrasting the foodways of major civilizations and cultures.

title: Food and the City Since 1800
description: This course examines the social and cultural history of various types of urban food markets in Western Europe and the United States from the late eighteenth century to the present.

title: History of Horses and Humans
description: A survey of the interactions between humans and horses beginning with ancient origins and extending into local history.

title: Senior Seminar
description: A capstone course for History majors.  Survey of literature and practical experience in methods and sources of history through bibliographical study and research papers.  Senior standing or by permission.

title: History of Appalachia
description: This course will explore the historical and historiographical development of Appalachia and the economic, political, and cultural forces that have shaped the lives and communities of mountaineers.

title: Amer Diplomacy 1900-Pres
description: American foreign relations in the 20th century. The gradual retreat from isolation in the period between World War I and World War II and modern American involvement in international commitments are stressed.

title: Tudor & Stuart England
description: A history of England under the Tudors and Stuarts, focusing primarily on demographic, social, cultural, and political developments.

title: Sex and Sexuality in the US
description: Examines the history of sex and sexuality in North America in the context of cultural, legal, economic, political and social history from the 16th century to present.

title: American Revolution
description: A varied view of the American Revolution and its impact on the American people.

title: Am Civil War & Reconstruc
description: A discussion of the economic, political, social, and cultural differences leading to the Civil War, the war itself, and an analysis of the political and economic importance of Reconstruction.

title: U.S. West to 1900
description: A study of the social, cultural, political, and economic history of the US West to 1900, along with West's place in public memory.

title: Renaissance & Reformation
description: The impact of the Renaissance upon esthetic, economic, and political developments especially in the 15th and 16th centuries.  The decline of Catholicism and the growth of the Protestant movement, and the influence of the two movements upon each other are stressed.

title: European Hist 1814-1914
description: A century of European political, economic and social history and its relationship to and influence upon the history of other world areas is noted. The impact of imperialistic rivalry is emphasized.

title: World War II
description: This course explores the Second World War.  It probes the nexus of diplomacy, politics, ideology, military strategy and operations, economics, and technological innovation that generated a truly global "total war."

title: Age of Revolutions in Europe
description: A survey of European history through the study of various revolutions.

title: Russia to 1917
description: A survey of Russian history to 1917 which examines Russia before the Russians, Kievan Russia, Appanage Russia, Muscovite Russia and Imperial Russia.

title: Soviet Russia and Beyond
description: Rise and fall of the Soviet Union, with emphasis on political and economic changes and Soviet foreign policy, and including an examination of the aftermath of the Soviet Union's collapse.

title: In Our Time-Am Since 1945
description: A study of America since World War II, focusing mainly on domestic politics, foreign affairs, the civil rights movement, the rise of minorities, and the fragmentation of American society.

title: The Vietnam War
description: A study of the origin and escalation of American involvement in Vietnam, the domestic impact of the war within the United States and the collapse of the South Vietnamese government.

title: Modern Japan
description: Begins with the samurai revolution of the nineteenth century Japan and explores the rise of Japan to the position of world power.

title: Modern China
description: This course will provide an overview of Chinese history in the modern era (1600 to present), including the major political, cultural, social and intellectual events and trends of this period.

title: Seminar in Public History
description: Introduction to the basic theories, ideas, and approaches to the application of historical theory or methods to projects presented to non-student publics; local and economic development applications and projects emphasized.

title: Stuff and Things in History
description:
This course investigates the rich potential of"things" -- objects, landscapes,
buildings, household utensils, furniture, foods, works of art, clothing, etc -- as
sources of insight about American history and culture.

title: West Virginia History
description: An interdisciplinary study of the state, its people and its institutions within the national context.

title: Arab-Israeli Conflict
description: This course will examine the historical developments of the modern Arab-Israeli conflict, with emphasis placed on political, socioeconomic, and cultural change and the prospects for peace.

title: Film and Empire
description: This course explores the nature and importance of empires through the reading of key texts and the study of selected films.

title: British History in Film
description: This course explores selected aspects of British history through the study of films and key texts.

title: World War I in Film
description: This course explores the origins, course, and meaning of World War I (1914-1918) through the use of selected films and readings.

title: History of Sports in America
description: Examines the effects of sports on American society and culture.  It explores the historical development of American sports and examine relationships between sports, nationalism, politics, economics, gender and American expansion.

title: The Kennedy Assassination
description: This course explores the events surrounding the assassination of President John F. Kennedy.

title: History of Ireland
description: This course explores the history of Ireland from the Middle Ages to the Present.

title: Intro to Genealogy
description: This course offers a hands-on introduction to genealogical research methods, tools, and ethics. Students will interpret family histories using census records, oral histories, military files etc.

title: Spies in History
description: This course explores the role played by spies and espionage in modern history.

title: The Viking World
description: By analyzing saga literature, poetry, and artifacts, students will examine the history of medieval Scandinavia.

title: Celtic Britain
description: This course examines the history, literature, and languages of the peoples who lived on the fringes of ancient and medieval Britain.

title: Medieval Britain
description: An examination of mainland Britain from the pre-Roman era through the Hundred Years War.

title: Medieval Queens
description: The evolution of power and rule from the late Roman Empire through medieval Europe, with a special focus on royal women.

title: Fashion and Textile History
description: Cross-cultural comparisons of textiles as primary sources for prehistoric cultures through the 1920's.

title: The Civil War in Myth & Memory
description: In a small seminar-setting, this course will look at Union and Confederate (northern and southern), Black and white, male and female interpretations of the war between 1865 and the present.

title: Tracks through Time
description: This course analyzes how railroads impacted global political, economic, social, and cultural development since 1800. It intertwines the local history of Huntington with global stories from Africa to China.

title: History Internship
description: History internships allow students to obtain real-world and hand-on job experience working with community partners.

title: Second Year Seminar
description:
An interdisciplinary seminar for second-year honors students that uses 
reflective inquiry to explore the interrelation and integration of leadership, ethics 
and civic engagement in a diverse, interconnected and changing world.

title: Yeager Seminar I
description: Interdisciplinary seminar for first semester Yeager scholars.

title: Yeager Seminar II
description: Interdisciplinary seminar for second semester Yeager scholars.

title: Yeager Seminar III
description: Interdisciplinary seminar for third semester Yeager scholars.

title: Yeager Seminar IV
description: Interdisciplinary seminar for fourth semester Yeager scholars.

title: Honors Peer Mentors & Leaders
description: An experiential learning course providing peer advisor and leadership training for work with college staff to provide student support services, including community building events.  May be repeated for credit.  (PR: Admitted Honors College 1)

title: Honors Engagement & Outreach
description: An experiential learning course providing outreach and leadership training. Students engage in recruitment, student support, and community-building while developing durable skills. May be repeated for credit. (Course can be repeated an unlimited amount of times.)

title: Special Topics
description: A study of special topics not listed under current course offerings.

title: Special Topics
description: A study of special topics not listed under current course offerings.

title: Special Topics
description: A study of special topics not listed under current course offerings.

title: Honors College Newsletter
description: An experiential learning course where students peer teach skills for and participate in information gathering, writing, editing, and designing the Honors College newsletter.  May be repeated for credit.

title: Student Assoc Steering Comm
description: Steering Committee is an experiential learning course in which students peer teach leadership and community building while organizing service, educational, and social events.  May be repeated for credit.

title: Honors Internship
description: Mentored experiential learning opportunity contracted with a business enterprise, professional organization, or government agency and the Honors College for honors students in good standing.  Requires mentor evaluation and student report.

title: TedxMarshallU Honors Interns
description: An experiential learning course conducted as a team internship with faculty mentor and TED licensee to plan and conduct the TEDxMarshallU event. May be repeated for credit.

title: Work Design
description: Design of work systems and measurement of work.  Students are also introduced to ergonomics considerations in the design of manual operations.

title: System Engineering
description: This course introduces students to the fundamental principles of systems engineering and their application to the development of complex systems.

title: Modeling & Quan Analysis
description: Simulation of complex deterministic/stochastic systems.  Random number generation.  Input and output analyses.  Spreadsheet simulations Design of simulation experiments.  Applications in manufacturing, supply-chain, networks, military, health care, service systems.

title: Quality Control
description: A comprehensive coverage of modern quality control techniques to include the design of statistical process control systems, lean 6 sigma, and process improvement.

title: Intro to Info Lit Research
description: Introduces students to basic research (including the use of the library and other resources), information literacy and information management skills needed to succeed in college, the online environment, and beyond.

title: RBA Career Preparation
description: Preparation for career after completing a college degree, including self-assessment, career exploration in conjunction  with Marshall's Career Services, development of a resume, cover letter, and completion of mock interview.

title: Adv Digital Literacy Skills
description: Examination of online search/retrieval skills used to define, access, evaluate, manage, integrate, create, and use digital information ethically; emphasis on critical thinking and practical use of Web 2.0 tools.

title: Instructional Technology Apps
description: Utilization of various computer software applications with emphasis on practical usage to produce graphic aids such as charts, brochures, spreadsheets, drawings and audio or video clips.

title: Intro to New Literacies
description: This course is an introduction to new literacies within the field of library science discussing meta, digital and visual literacy information trends and the current issues affecting these literacy types.

title: RBA Adv Portfolio Development
description: Students are coached through the RBA portfolio development process.  This includes:  What a portfolio is; inventorying their work and life experience; and creating and submitting the portfolio.

title: Digital Citizenship
description: Introduction to skills that build digital citizenship literacy that will help students learn the cognitive, social and technical skills for navigating and evaluating the digital information ecosystem.

title: Library Organiza & Admin
