title: Applied Music Laboratory
description: A forum devoted to the development of applied music skills, supplying the opportunity for music majors both to demonstrate performance skills and to observe the skills of colleagues.

title: Developmental Class Piano
description: Class instruction for students with little or no prior background in piano. Preparation for entry into the MUS 179 sequence.  Pre: Permission.

title: Critical Thinking Music (CT)
description: Includes the study of critical thinking in music fields with specific focus on music education and performance.

title: Elementary Music Theory I
description: Study of the diatonic harmony of the Common Practice Period through development of compositional and analytical skills. Emphasis on cadences, melodic form, non-harmonic tones and diatonic triads.

title: Elem Aural Skills I
description: Sight-singing of melodies, and dictation of harmony, rhythm, and melody using elementary rhythms and diatonic pitch materials.

title: Music in Society
description: Exploration of the roles and value of music in culture and society.  Development of musical awareness through mastery of basic terminology, stylistic concepts, and critical listening skills.  For non-music majors.

title: Music in Society Honors
description: Exploration of the roles an value of music in culture and society.  Development of musical awareness through mastery of basic terminology, stylistic concepts, and critical listening skills.  For non-music majors.  3 credits.

title: Class Voice
description: Classes for voice minors and electives designed for beginners.

title: Class Piano
description: Classes for piano minors and electives progressing from beginner to proficiency level.

title: Class Piano
description: Classes for piano minors and electives progressing from beginner to proficiency level.

title: Class Piano
description: Classes for piano minors and electives progressing from beginner to proficiency level.

title: Introduction to World Music
description:
This course will survey native musics of Africa, Asia and the Americas as an
aspect of culture.  No formal background in music is required.

title: A Cappella Ensemble
description:
A mixed a capella ensemble dedicated to the performance of a variety of popular, jazz, and contemporary sytles.  Open to all students regardless of major.
PR: Permission.

title: Intro to Electronic Music (CT)
description: A non-technical introduction to the theory, practice and literature of electronic music.  This course fulfills a Core I/CT Course requirement.  Open to all majors.

title: Advanced Music Theory I
description: Study of advanced harmonic concepts in tonal music including modulation, altered pre-dominants, and chromatic mediant relationships.

title: Advanced Music Theory II
description: The study of musical forms and formal processes found in music of the late 18th through the 19th centuries.

title: Adv Aural Skills I
description: Sight-singing and aural analysis of melodies, harmonies, and rhythms found in 18th and 19th century music, including modulating melodies, secondary dominants, multi-part harmonic dictation, and characteristic rhythms. (PRE: MUS 112 and MUS 114; MUS 211 must be taken concurrently or prior to enrollment in MUS 213 )

title: Adv Aural Skills II
description: Sight-singing and aural analysis of 19th and 20th century music, including chromatic harmony, modulating melodies, multi-part harmonic dictation, scale/chord identification, characteristic 20th century sonorities  and advanced rhythmic materials. (PREREQ: MUS 211 and MUS 213 ; MUS 212 must be taken concurrently or prior to enrollment in MUS 213 )

title: Jazz Theory
description: Fundamental jazz theory and ear training. Chords, scales, nomenclature. Harmonic progressions and substitutions. Aural recognition of melodic and chord structures and functions. Composition and transcription. Pre: MUS 215

title: Ital & Eng Diction Singer
description: A systematic study of the pronunciation and problems encountered by singers when performing repertoire with English and Italian texts.

title: Fren & Ger Diction Singer
description: A systematic study of the pronunciation and problems encountered by singers when performing repertoire with French and German texts.

title: History of Popular Music
description: A chronological listening survey course of popular music with an emphasis on examining the intersection of musical, historical, sociological, and cultural factors.

title: Jazz Improvisation I
description: Introduction to improvisation. Diatonic chord/scale relationships. Basic forms. Melodic construction and elaboration. Rhythmic improvisation. Keys and modes. Pre: MUS 116

title: Jazz Improvisation II
description: Continued development of skills and techniques. Idiomatic jazz patterns. Survey of standard literature. Turnarounds, blues progressions. Transcription of solos. Performance and elaboration of representative literature. Pre: MUS 231

title: Percussion Class
description: A class of non-music majors who will learn to play percussion instruments from various cultures.

title: Music Comp/Theory Sem
description: Introduction to advanced theoretical and compositional methods employed by artist/researchers in analyzing music from the 19th and 20th centuries. (PR: MUS 215, Permission)

title: History of Jazz
description: The history of the development of jazz and related forms from the 19th century antecedents to recent experimental trends.

title: Musical Prods Practicum
description: Application of musical production processes, theories and techniques.  Specialized practical experience in all musical aspects of rehearsal and performance.  Each number may be taken four times.

title: Guitar Techniques
description: The study of guitar techniques that will allow students to play and teach guitar at a basic level in a public school music program.

title: Jazz Piano Class
description: Class instruction progressing from beginner to advanced. Development of literature and skills needed to pass the jazz piano proficiency exam. (Prerequisites: Permission. Permission granted upon completion of piano proficiency or completion of jazz piano placement exam.)

title: Jazz Piano Class
description: Class instruction progressing from beginner to advanced. Development of literature and skills needed to pass the jazz proficiency exam. (Prerequisites: Permission. Permission granted upon completion of piano proficiency or completion of jazz piano placement exam.)

title: Jazz Piano Class
description: Class instruction progressing from beginner to advanced. Development of literature and skills needed to pass the jazz piano proficiency exam. (Prerequisites: Permission. Permission granted upon completion of piano proficiency or completion of jazz piano placement exam.)

title: Jazz Piano Class
description: Class instruction progressing from beginner to advanced. Development of literature and skills needed to pass the jazz piano proficiency exam. (Prerequisites: Permission. Permission granted upon completion of piano proficiency or completion of jazz piano placement exam.)

title: Music History to 1750
description: A study of the history and development of music through 1750, including the interaction of music with historical events, world cultures, other arts and philosophy. PR: MUS 211 and 213 with a grade of C or better

title: Analysis
description: Analysis of music from the 18th through 20th centuries using general methodology, set and serial theory.

title: Advanced Analysis
description: Analysis of musical works from the late 19th century through the present era using sight and sound.

title: Styles
description: An investigation of the distinguishing characteristics of the music of major composers by the study, dissection and comparison of major works.

title: Music and Sound in Film
description: A study of the role of music and sound in film.  Development of terminology and skills for analyzing and interpreting sound tracks of films throughout the history of film.

title: Vocal Techniques
description: Foundation principles of voice usage, interpretation, and problems of vocal pedagogy.  For instrumental music major students.  Courses must be taken in sequence.

title: Basic Conducting
description: Fundamental conducting skills: baton use, beat patterns of simple/compound meters, fermata holds/releases, cues to discrete parts using hand, visual gestures, leadership, conducting terminology, transportation and score reading.

title: Counterpoint
description: Eighteenth Century counterpoint includes creative writing in this style and analysis of contrapuntal composition of this period based upon principles learned in introductory theory courses.

title: Instrumental Arranging
description: The study of the instruments of the modern orchestra, their history, technical possibilities and limitations, and practical application of technique in public school work.

title: Orchestration
description: A detailed study of band and orchestral instrument capabilities and their use in various large and small ensembles. (PR: MUS 212 and MUS 214 )

title: Jazz Arranging/Composing
description: Fundamental techniques and analysis of jazz masterworks applied to composing and arranging in various instrumental and vocal jazz styles. Orchestration for large and small ensembles. Pre: MUS 307

title: Music Industry Law
description: An introduction to the music industry and relevant areas of contract, copyright, and trademark law.

title: Music Business I
description: A study of the intersection of music and business aimed at developing foundational skills and an understanding of the structure of the music business.

title: Jazz Improvisation III
description: Advanced skills and techniques. Extended forms, substitute harmonic progressions. Survey of standard bebop literature. Transcription of solos. Performance and elaboration of representative literature. Pre: MUS 232

title: Jazz Improvisation IV
description: Advanced skills, techniques, and performance practices. Compound and extended forms. Survey of post-bebop, free jazz, fusion, and Afro-Carribbean literature. Transcription of solos. Performance of representative literature. Pre: MUS 331

title: Mat & Meth School Mus Pre K-4
description: Elementary music education techniques, including movement, song, dance, rhythm, and musicianship.  Study of Orff, Kodaly, Dalcroze techniques.  Curriculum design and incorporation of music curriculum into elementary curriculum.  Field experience required.  (PR: EDF 218 and EDF 319 )

title: Mat & Meth Instru Mus 5-12
description: Study of curriculum development in the middle and secondary schools and appropriate instrumental materials and methods of presentation of music in grades 5-12.

title: Mat & Meth Chor Grn Mus 5-12
description: Intensive study of vocal/choral materials.  Methods of presentation of music in middle and secondary grades. Curriculum design and incorporation of music curriculum into general curriculum.  Field experience required. (PR: EDF 218 and EDF 319 )

title: Mus in Elementary Classroom
description: Music in the Elementary Classroom.  Development of fundamental music reading and performing skills.  Materials, procedures, and systems to teach music in grades PreK-6. Relationship of music activities to general classroom curriculum.

title: Guitar Literature I
description: A survey of the literature for the guitar from c. 1400 to the end of the nineteenth century.  (PR: Permission)

title: Guitar Pedagogy I
description: A survey of guitar pedagogy literature, and a practicum in teaching classical guitar.  (PR: Permission)

title: Music History 1730 - 1900
description: A study of the history and development of music c. 1730 to c. 1900, including the interaction of music with historical events, world cultures, other arts and philosophy  PR: MUS 290 or MUS 260 (Jazz Studies majors only) with a grade of C or better

title: Music History Since 1900
description: A study of the history and development of music since c. 1900, including the interaction of music with historical events, world cultures, other arts and philosophy  PR: MUS 360 with a grade of C or better

title: Fife and Drum Corps
description: Performing, marching, uniformed ensemble devoted to the music of the American Revolution and Chief Justice John Marshall. Instruments featured include fifes and drums.

title: Advanced Class Piano
description: Class instruction for experienced students. Development of repertoire ensemble skills, and sightreading. Pre: Completion of MUS 179D, or completion of piano proficiency, or permission of instructor.

title: Collaborative Composition
description: Music composition projects proposed, developed, and realized with collaborators in disciplines outside of music including but not exclusive to: visual art/media, theatre, film/video, dance, or video games (PR: Instructor permission)

title: Research in Music
description: Basic research procedures and bibliography study culminating in a project in the student's area of specialization.

title: Chamber Choir
description: Advanced, auditioned choral ensemble open to all university students.  Repertoire performed locally and on tour includes great chamber literature of the past five centuries.  Three rehearsals per week.

title: Jazz Pedagogy and Conducting
description: Methods and materials for jazz curriculum.  Ensemble organization, rehearsal and conducting techniques.  Selection of literature and equipment.  Organizations and presentation of public performances. PR: MUS 323

title: Jazz Styles
description: Structural forms used in jazz, analysis of extended forms.  Techniques for solo transcriptions.  Harmonic practices in specific styles.  Survey of significant jazz composers, performers, genres. PR: MUS 217

title: Music Psy and Learning Theory
description: Introduction to the psychology of music, human development, and assessment of learning.

title: Advanced Conducting
description: Advanced study of choral and instrumental conducting techniques emphasizing rehearsal and performance strategies, score study, ensemble warm-ups, asymmetric and changing meters.  Lab ensemble experience provided.

title: Vocal Pedagogy
description: Review of materials, concepts and methodology used in teaching singing; overview of anatomy and function of the voice.  Emphasis on beginning and intermediate levels.

title: Teaching Advanced Piano
description: Materials and techniques of presentation; appropriate selection of repertoire; teaching strategies for late intermediate and advanced levels; exploration of pedagogical, business, and wellness resource for the independent piano teacher.

title: Teachin Beg-Intermed Piano
description: Materials and techniques of presentation; development of reading skills; basic fundamentals of technique; cultivation of musicianship.  Emphasis is on elementary and intermediate levels.

title: Guitar Literature II
description: A survey of the literature for the guitar during the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.  (PR: Permission)

title: Guitar Pedagogy II
description: A survey of guitar pedagogy literature, and a practicum in teaching classical guitar.

title: Cello Ensemble
description: Cello students will rehearse and perform works from the major literature for cello ensemble.

title: Flute Ensemble
description: Performs a wide variety of musical styles from full flute choir to quarters, trios, etc.  Membership required of all flute majors; others by audition.  One rehearsal per week.

title: Musical Prods Practicum
description: Application of musical production processes, theories and techniques. Specialized practical experience in all musical aspects of rehearsal and performance.  Each number may be taken four times.

title: Applied Pedagogy Lab
description: Practical application of concepts, methodology, and materials covered to demonstrate efficient and effective teaching in the applied area.

title: Musical Theatre Capstone
description: A culminating Musical Theatre performance and critical self-reflection. Students develop proposals with input from faculty mentor.

title: Capstone Project in Music
description: A discipline-based experience designed to combine classroom and studio education in a summarizing project. (PR: MUS 310, successful completion of piano proficiency requirements)

title: BFA Composition Capstone
description: A discipline-based experience designed to combine classroom and studio education in a summarizing project.

title: Chamber Choir
description: Advanced, auditioned choral ensemble open to all university students.  Repertoire performed locally and on tour includes great chamber literature of the past five centuries.  Three rehearsals per week. May be repeated for credit.

title: University Chorus
description: A mixed chorus of 60-90 singers open to all university students without audition. Public performances of a variety of music are given each semester. Three rehearsals per week.

title: Choral Union
description: Large choral ensemble available to university and regional singers without audition. Gives public performances or oratorios and works for chorus and orchestra twice a year. One rehearsal per week.

title: Opera Workshop
description: Preparation and performance of opera scenes and full operas. Membership open to students as singers, pianists, and technical personnel. Roles assigned by audition. Two rehearsals per week plus private coaching.

title: 3rd Avenue
description:
A mixed a capella ensemble dedicated to the performance of a variety of popular, jazz, and contemporary styles. Open to all students regardless of major. May be repeated for credit. 
PR: Permission.

title: Orchestra
description: The Marshall Community Orchestra is open to all university students, faculty, and interested musicians in the community with permission of the instructor. Concerts are presented each semester.

title: Wind Symphony
description: A select, auditioned group of wind and percussion players who study and perform original and transcribed works for wind orchestra and works for small mixed chamber ensembles. (PR: Audition)

title: Symphonic Band
description: The Marshall University Symphonic Band rehearses twice a week and presents two concerts each semester. Symphonic Band performs a varied repertoire of music from wind band staples to contemporary works.

title: Concert Band
description: A concert band for any undergraduate student who has had experience playing a band instrument in high school or middle school. May be repeated for credit.

title: Marching Thunder
description: The Marching Thunder rehearses three times a week and performs a varied selection of music every home game as well as exhibitions around the country. May be repeated 6 semesters.

title: Sixth Man Band
description: The Marshall University Sixth Man Band performs at all home men's and women's basketball games. The band provides music to enhance the overall game experience for players and fans.

title: Jazz Ensemble
description: Study and performance of traditional and progressive jazz repertoire in a big band ensemble. Open to all university students by audition. May be repeated for credit.

title: Jazz Improv Ensemble
description: Ensemble improvisation from duet to tentet. Emphasis on music sightreading, recognition and application of chord/ scale relationships in a performance setting. Ensemble playing skills. May be repeated for credit.

title: Soul Stampede
description: Ensemble focusing on performance of popular songs rooted in Black American Music traditions. Emphasis on common practices of commercial musicians/performers, stage comportment, and ensemble technique. May be repeated for credit.

title: Brass Band of the TriState
description: A community/university ensemble modeled after the British-style brass band. May be repeated for credit.

title: Contemporary Music Ens
description: Performance of contemporary music in various media. May be repeated for credit. Counts towards improvisation requirement.

title: Collegium Musicum
description: An ensemble for the performance of Medieval, Renaissance, and Baroque music, with special attention to performance practices and instrumental and vocal techniques. May be repeated for credit.

title: African Drum & Dance Ens
description: The African Drumming and Dance Ensemble specializes in performing traditional music of West Africa. This ensemble combines dancing, drumming and singing into a rich cultural experience in non-western music. The ensemble is open to all university students and does not require prior musical training.

title: Woodwind Ensemble
description: Chamber ensemble experience for woodwind players. May be repeated for credit.

title: Brass Ensemble
description: Chamber ensemble experience for brass players. May be repeated for credit.

title: Percussion Ensemble
description: An ensemble dedicated to performing a wide variety of musical styles on instruments in the percussion family. May be repeated for credit.

title: String Ensemble
description: Chamber ensemble experience for string players. May be repeated for credit.

title: Piano Ensemble
description: An ensemble elective for piano majors and qualified piano secondary/elective students. Perform a wide variety of musical styles for multiple pianists on 1-5 pianos. (PR: Audition with instructor.)

title: Guitar Ensemble
description: An ensemble for guitar majors and qualified guitar elective students; focus on sight reading skills, ensemble accuracy and position playing. May be repeated for credit.

title: Basic Musicianship
description:
Study of music fundamentals and aural skills including: notation, key signatures, scales, intervals, and triads and aural recognition of meter, scales,
intervals, and triads. Online section open to non-music majors.

title: Mus Theatre Musicianship Lab
description: A music literacy lab for the musical theatre major emphasizing the practices of sight-singing and rhythm skills covered in MUS 101.

title: The Professional Musician
description: Investigation of traditional and non-traditional music career options and the role of the musician in arts advocacy, community engagement, and arts education. (pre-requisities: none)

title: Elementary Music Theory I
description: Study of the diatonic harmony of the Common Practice Period through development of compositional and analytical skills. Emphasis on cadences, melodic form, non-harmonic tones and diatonic triads.

title: Elementary Music Theory II
description: Continued study of diatonic harmony of the Common Practice Period through development of compositional and analytical skills. Introduction to elementary forms, elementary modulation, and secondary dominants.

title: Elem Aural Skills I
description: Sight-singing of melodies, and dictation of harmony, rhythm, and melody using elementary rhythms and diatonic pitch materials.

title: Elem Aural Skills II
description: Sight-singing of melodies, and dictation of harmony, rhythm, and melody using diatonic pitch materials in major and minor modes. Introduction to syncopation, secondary dominants, and elementary forms.

title: Class Piano I
description: Classes for piano minors and electives progressing from beginner to proficiency level.

title: Class Piano II
description: Classes for piano minors and electives progressing from beginner to proficiency level.

title: Class Piano III
description: Classes for piano minors and electives progressing from beginner to proficiency level.

title: Class Piano IV
description: Classes for piano minors and electives progressing from beginner to proficiency level.

title: Class Voice
description: Classes for voice minors and electives; designed for beginners. May be repeated for credit.

title: Class Guitar
description: Beginning guitar instruction for elective students. May be repeated for credit.

title: Aural Perceptions of Music Lit
description: The development of aural skills in perceiving timbre, texture, rhythm, meter, linear organization, harmonic organization, and form through listening to selected works.

title: Intro to Music Industry
description: An introduction to the music industry and music production, covering recording, music publishing, live performance, and artist management, while highlighting the roles of artists, songwriters, producers, managers, lawyers, and agents.

title: Intro to Music Technology
description: Introduction to music technology used for sound reinforcement, digital audio re-cording and editing, music notation, and MIDI data.

title: Digital Recording I
description: Concepts, implementation, and utlization of digital audio workstation software and hardware: MIDI, digital audio, recording techniques, production, and using the Internet to empower music creators (open to non-music majors)

title: Music Entrepreneurship
description: Skills required to create careers as entrepreneurial performing artists or to supplement an arts career in entrepreneurial pursuits, including planning, self-promotion, networking, audience building, and marketing.

title: Music Industry Law
description: An introduction to the music industry and relevant areas of contract, copyright, and trademark law.

title: Music Business I
description: A study of the intersection of music and business aimed at developing foundational skills and an understanding of the structure of the music business.

title: Digital Recording II
description: Study of intermediate digital audio workstation operation and techniques, including multitrack mixing workflow, signal flow basics, miking strategies, digital audio theory, and session planning.

title: Studio Recording Techniques
description: In-depth study of studio recording techniques, with emphasis on console operation, mic selection and operation, signal flow, recording session planning, and studio etiquette and workflow.

title: Music Business II
description: An in-depth examination of the commerical music industry designed at developing business related competencies including entrepreneurship, IP management, legal issues, and contract law; skills essential for maintaining a professional music career.

title: Advanced Music Production
description: Music production techniques for a variety of genres using a digital audio workstation. Focus on large-scale multitrack sessions. Successful completion confers Avid Pro Tools Specialist Certification.

title: Music Industry Capstone
description: Students will synthesize concepts of music industry study to create a capstone project.

title: Music Internship
description: Practical synthesis and application of knowledge and skills gained during the student's course of study.

title: Living Systems
description: This course is designed to equip students to use the process of scientific inquiry to answer questions related to living systems.

title: Living Systems Lab
description: Laboratory course to accompany NRE 111 Living Systems.

title: Discussion in Environ Sci (CT)
description: Critical thinking course designed to examine and explore issues in environmental science including protection of terrestrial and aquatic resources and production of energy and food for a growing population.

title: Introduction to Agriculture
description: This course will discuss scientific concepts essential to agriscience and examine practices, careers, and new directions in various fields in agriculture.

title: Energy
description: The course introduces the students to the properties and the interfaces of biological and physical systems with emphasis on energy concepts, production and distribution in both systems.

title: Human Dimensions Nat Res (CT)
description: This course provides an overview of human-nature relationship from the perspective of conservation and natural resource management.

title: Special Topics
description: A course covering topics not treated in regular course offerings.

title: Special Topics

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study for selected sophomores or advanced freshman under supervision of faculty; may be repeated only once.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study for selected sophomores or advanced freshman under supervision of faculty; may be repeated only once.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study for selected sophomores or advanced freshman under supervision of faculty; may be repeated only once.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study for selected sophomores or advanced freshman under supervision of faculty; may be repeated only once.

title: Principles of Soil Science
description: This course will examine the principles of soils, including origin, composition, classification, and physical, chemical, and biological properties and processes, as well as agricultural and environmental applications.

title: Principles of Soil Science Lab
description: A laboratory course that demonstrates the application of concepts introduced in NRE 300 Principles of Soil Science.

title: Animal Production
description: This course will examine the principles of animal production through the application of anatomy, physiology, behavior, nutrition, reproduction, breeding, and genetics in the production and management of farm animals.

title: Nature Enviro Problems
description: The effects of human activity on ecological, political, economic, and cultural systems are examined.  Particular attention is given to present human population growth, industrial activities, and energy availability.

title: Resol Environ Problems
description: Students examine case studies of current environmental problems and propose methods of remediation.  Cultural, political, economic, as well as ecological and physiographic factors are considered.

title: Assess I: Terrestrial Systems
description: Use of scientific procedure and current technology to characterize and quantify sensitive elements of terrestrial ecosystems and to assess human impact on these systems.

title: Assessment II: Aquatic Ecology
description: Use of scientific procedures and current technology to characterize and quantify sensitive elements of aquatic ecosystems and to assess human impact on these systems.

title: Soil Fertility/Plant Nutrition
description: This course will examine properties of soil fertility, its relationship to plan nutrition, and practices in nutrient management and fertilizer application.

title: Horticulture
description: This course will examine the principles and practices in horticulture and apply these practices through a semester service project.

title: Sustainable Agriculture
description: This course will examine the principles of sustainable agriculture and its relationship with natural resources while identifying challenges to agricultural sustainability and frontiers in the field.

title: Agricultural Entomology
description: Globally, insects have tremendous impacts on agriculture.  This course introduces students to insects and their roles in agriculture including integrated pest management and pollination ecology.

title: Compost Science & Operations
description: An advanced exploration of composting systems, evaluation methods, sustainable management strategies, compost quality characteristics and analysis, and community, environmental, and operational aspects of compost production and utilization.

title: GIS and Data Systems
description: Course focuses on the relationships among the scientific method, data structures, and geographic images.  Students relate hypothesis formation and databases through the development of ARCMap documents.

title: Water Policy and Regulations
description: Examination of how aquatic resources are protected for humans and species of concern by current regulatory framework.

title: Aquatic Toxicology
description: This course will introduce students to the principles of aquatic toxicology including regulations driving biological criteria, development of laboratory toxicity testing and test methodology.

title: Biomonitoring
description: Biomonitoring is the use of organisms to assess habitat and water quality of a stream.  Current aquatic biomonitoring focuses on the utilization of benthic invertebrates and fishes communities.

title: Fisheries Management
description: The history, regulations, theory, and science behind the management/mismanagement of inland fisheries.

title: Sustainable Tropical Ecology
description: This course is designed to introduce the cultural components, human impacts, basic ecology, habitats types, biodiversity, plant communities, life histories, and conservation of tropical ecosystems.

title: Sustain Tropical Travel Lab
description: Laboratory companion course to NRE 445 . Investigation of aquatic and terrestrial biota in the tropical environment and examination threats to tropical ecosystems. International trip, with an extra fee, required.

title: Sustainable Tropical Lab
description: Laboratory companion course to NRE 445 . Investigation of aquatic and terrestrial biota in the tropical environment and examination threats to tropical ecosystems.

title: Aquatic Invertebrates
description: A survey course of the freshwater invertebrates found in the eastern United States. This course focuses on the taxonomy, life histories, and ecology of crustaceans, mollusks, and insects.

title: ES Internship
description: A supervised internship in an area of natural resources and the environment.

title: ES/NRRM Capstone Prep
description: Prepares students for the senior project, internship experience, and for careers beyond graduation.  Life skills are introduced by building on communication, organization, and project management skills (capstone).

title: ES Senior Capstone
description: Students develop and complete a research project under the direction of a faculty member in the NRE department (capstone).

title: Intro Natural Res & Rec Mgmt
description: An orientation to the profession and its settings, emphasizing history, trends, concepts, and relationships to other fields.

title: Outdoor Leadership:  Canoeing
description: This course is designed to give students the skills essential for the pursuit of employment in guiding participants on flat-water canoe courses in backcountry settings.

title: Outdoor Leadership:  Fly Fish
description: This course is designed to give students the skills essential for the pursuit of being able to guide participants on fly fishing excursions in the backcountry.

title: Outdoor Leadership: Bass Fish
description: This course is designed to give students the skills essential to pursue employment as a guide on bass fishing excursions in the backcountry.

title: Outdoor Leadership: Backpack
description: This course is designed to give students the foundation skills essential in the pursuit of being leading participants on backcountry backpacking experiences.  Leave No Trace ethics will be taught.

title: Analytical Methods: Statistics
description: Students develop an understanding of statistical reasoning through the use of software to generate, summarize, and draw conclusions from data.  Course enhances statistical technique dexterity through analysis of applied problems.

title: Nature Study
description: Designed to provide students with the fundamental understanding of and for the delivery of nature based educational programs offered through an experiential framework.

title: Intro to Outdoor Recreation
description: Organization, administration, and delivery of outdoor recreation activities and resources.  Emphasis upon federal, state, and local government programs and areas.

title: Environmental Interpretation
description: Principles and techniques of environmental interpretation as practiced in federal, state, and private agencies.

title: Intro to Environmental Educ
description: This course is the study of environmental education, its foundations, emergence in the 1960's, its evolution, the systems approach to it, and the application of it in the field.

title: Sports & Campus Rec Mgmt
description: This course will deal with the fields of recreational sports and campus recreation management.  It will present the foundations of both fields, the development, implementation, and trends in today's programs.

title: Concepts Philosophies in NRRM
description: A systematic approach to the concepts and philosophies for managing wildland, wilderness, and protected areas.

title: Special Event Managment
description: This course will study the processes for event facilitation.  Special attention will be given to the roles and skills utilized by a variety of recreation managers.

title: Adventure Education Leadership
description: This course focuses on preparing students to be outdoor adventure education leaders, facilitating programs in both the front and back country utilizing adventure education techniques in an outdoor setting.

title: Tourism Planning & Management
description: The course will examine the critical issues addressed by tourism planning and management, which are the positive and negative influences of tourism on the destination's economy, society, culture, and environment.

title: Visitor Behavior in NRRM
description: This course provides an overview and analysis of individual and group behavior as it pertains to consumer activity in the context of recreation and tourism resource environments.

title: Nature-based Tourism Mgmt
description: This course will examine the theoretical foundations, applications and best management practices in nature-based tourism, including its sustainable development and economic, sociocultural, and environmental impacts.

title: Rec Resource Oper and Admin
description: Focused on the management, operation, and administration practices of recreation resource areas.  Includes supervision of personnel, budgeting, and public relations for a variety of park and public land organizational structures.

title: Leisure & Aging
description: A course presenting an overview of leisure services for the elderly.  Topics include research results, theories, and modern day trends.  A wellness model will be included.

title: Research Eval & Assess in NRRM
description: Theoretical & practical approach to research, evaluation and assessment of the social sciences of natural resources and recreational management.

title: Park & Recreation Ecology
description: The course is designed to help students identify and evaluate the level of resource impact, understand factors that cause impacts, and suggest management actions to minimize impacts under given conditions.

title: Maint of Nat Res & Rec
description: A study of the knowledge and skills necessary to supervise and administer the general development and maintenance of park and recreation areas and facilities.

title: Rec Resource Planning & Design
description: Basic considerations in the planning and design of natural areas, parks, forests, recreation, and sport area infrastructure, facilities and associated amenities.

title: Wilderness/Protected Area Mgmt
description: This course will examine the historic and current philosophies of wilderness and protected area management as applicable to NGOs, local, state, and federal land management programs.

title: GIS/RS in Natural Resources
description: Focusing on natural resource management, the course will explore techniques and procedures required for spatially explicit data analysis in park and protected area applications.

title: Construction OHV Trails
description: A course designed to instruct students in contemporary methods and techniques of constructing OHV trails and related facilities.

title: Internship
description: A supervised 40-hour per week, 6 week internship in which the students work with park and recreation agencies.  (PR:  NRRM major. Advisor approval required.)

title: Introduction to Nursing
description: Indroduce the nursing role and use of the nursing process in assisting adult clients to meet basic needs.  Clinical included.

title: Intro to Professional Nursing
description: Introduction to the concepts and processes basic to the nursing profession, including historical and legal aspects, career roles, medical terminology, and practical techniques to prepare for the baccalaureate nursing program.

title: Health Promotion Thru Lifespan
description: Study of nursing assessment of the individual through the life span in relation to wellness promotion and the impact of illness.

title: Health Alterations I
description: Focus is on nursing care of adult clients responding to potential and actual health alterations. Clinical included. (PR: NUR 120 )

title: Found of Prof Nursing I
description: Introduction to philosophical and theoretical foundations of nursing, exploration and integration of concepts and processes basic to professional nursing practice.  Laboratory included.

title: Found of Prof Nursing II
description: Introduction to professional nursing practice in relation to potential and simple alterations in health.  Practicum included.

title: Intro Nur Pharm
description:
Focus is on role of the Associate degree nurse in understanding the human response to
pharmacological therapy.

title: Psychiatric Nursing
description: Focus is on the nursing role in caring for clients with alterations of psychosocial functioning.  Clinical included. (PR: NUR 220 )

title: Health Alterations II
description: Focus is on nursing care of adult clients with health alterations of specific physiological systems.  Role requirements and processes utilized in managing groups of clients in introduced.  Clinical included.  (PR: NUR 220 ) 5 hours theory/ 2 hours lab.

title: Maternal-Child Nursing
description: Focus is on the nursing role utilized in promoting health and caring for the child bearing family and pediatric clients.  Clinical included.  (PR: NUR 220 )

title: Health Alterations III
description: focus is  on nursing care of adult clients with health alterations of specific physiological systems.  Clinical included.  (PR: NUR 230 )

title: Transition to Nursing Practice
description: Intro to the concepts of professional nursing practice of legal, ethical, theory, research, evidence based practice and health promotion.

title: Concepts in Prof Nursing
description: Emphasizes concepts and processes essential to professional nursing practice. Philosophical and theoretical foundations of nursing are examined. Focus is on professional role and role transition. (PR: Admission to RN to B.S.N. nursing program)

title: Fundamentals of Nursing Care
description: Introduction to foundations of nursing including potential and simple health alterations, exploration and integration of concepts, and processes of nursing practice.  Laboratory and clinical included.

title: Med-Surg Nursing I
description: Focus is on the nursing care of young adult to geriatric patients with potential and actual alterations in health.  Practicum included.

title: Family and Chronic Illness
description: Focus in family nursing theory related to human responses utilizing theoretical models for assessment and health promotion.  Included is consideration of the impact of chronic illness.

title: Phys Assess Across Lifespan
description: Development of skills in taking health history and performing physical examination of clients throughout the life span.  Practicum included.

title: Care of Childbearing Families
description: Focus of nursing is on the diagnosis and treatment of human responses to changes that occur in the expanding family. Practicum included.

title: Psych / Mental Health Nursing
description: Focus is upon nursing care of clients of all ages in relation to human responses to psychosocial problems.

title: Adult Nursing I
description: Focus is on nursing care of clients from young to geriatric adults responding to health problems. Practicum included.

title: Adult Nursing II
description: Focus is on nursing care of clients from young to geriatric adults responding to increasingly complex health care problems.  Practicum included.

title: Health Physical Assessment
description:
Focus on providing nurses with the knowledge base and clinical skills necessary to obtain in-depth health histories and complete physical assessments of clients of various ages and cultural backgrounds. 
(PR: admission to RN to B.S.N. nursing program or Permission)

title: Pharmacology for Nurses
description:
Focus in on the role of the nurse in drug therapy.  Specific drug classifications and
prototypical drugs, their actions, effects, and nursing  implications are described in-depth.

title: Transcultural Nursing
description: The focus is on health care practices and beliefs of a variety of cultures which interact with the United States health care system.  Transcultural nursing theories and concepts are addressed.

title: MGT and Leadership in Nursing
description: The study of professional nursing, leadership, and the management of patient care.

title: Pediatric Nursing
description:
Nursing Care of Children
Focus is on nursing care of pediatric clients of all ages and their family's response to common and complex health problems.  Practicum included.

title: Community Nursing for RN
description: Focus is on the foundations of community-oriented nursing practice for the Registered Nurse with an emphasis on health promotion and disease prevention.

title: Med-Surg Nursing II
description: Focus is on the nursing care of young adult to geriatric patients with acute health conditions requiring critical care interventions.  Practicum included.

title: Adult Nursing III
description: Focus is on nursing care of young to geriatric adults responding to health problems requiring critical care intervention.  Practicum included.

title: Intro Research EBP
description: Focus is on research as it relates to evidence based practice. This course addresses the steps of research and evidence based practice to provide a basis for nursing practice.

title: Contemporary Nursing
description: Focus on foundational knowledge to competently address nursing issues in an ever-evolving healthcare landscape.

title: Prof Nursing Practice
description:
Focus on nursing leadership, the management of patient care, and issues inherent to
professional nursing practice.

title: Community / Public Health Nurs
description: Focus is upon public health principles and nursing practice with opportunity to provide health promotion for clients at risk and long term care for individuals and families in the home.

title: Nursing Capstone Practicum
description: Focus in on leadership activities related to the roles of the professional nurse:  provider of care, coordinator of care, and member of the profession.  Practicum included.

title: NCLEX Success
description:
The purpose of this course is to assist the student to synthesize the content of the
baccalaureate nursing educational program.

title: Prof Engagement Nursing
description:
Focus is on evidence based practice and application of theoretical and empirical knowledge to address issues in management, health care policy, ethics, leadership, and health promotion of clients. 
(PR: NUR 305 and RN licensure; PR/CR: NUR 410 , NUR 416 , NUR 418 ))

description: Study of topics not available  in other courses.

title: Intro Phil Ancient Period (CT)
description: The origins of philosophical activity among the Greeks by means of a selective sounding of several major thinkers.

title: Intro Phil Ancient-Honors (CT)
description: A detailed consideration of selected texts from Ancient philosophy, such as the pre-Socratics, Plato, Aristotle, the Stoics, the Epicureans, the Academic Skeptics, and the neo-Platonists.

title: Intro Phil Modern Period (CT)
description: Questions and answers concerning the nature of existence and human values and how we come to know them.

title: Ethics of Health Care (CT)
description: A basic introduction to ethical theory, followed by an application of this theory to problems in health care.  For students in health care professions and those interested in applied ethics.

title: Philosophy & Human Exist (CT)
description: An introduction to philosophy drawing from both ancient and modern thinkers and texts.

title: AI and Ethics
description: We discuss what AI and technology are; how they systematically affect our encounters with, and understanding of, the world; how those encounters interact with values like equality, autonomy, progress.

title: Managing Life
description: Philosophical approaches to dealing with life's deep challenges and difficult moments, with the help of some of the great classics of world thought.

title: Humanities and Film (CT)
description: An interdisciplinary course to introduce students to the elements of a humanistic education through study of film.  (Same as Classics 250 and Religious Studies 250; PR or CR: ENG 101 )

title: Intro to Formal Logic (CT)
description: Introduction to three standard systems of formal logic: traditional categorical syllogistic logic, sentential logic, and first-order predicate logic.

title: Special Topics
description: Group or individual study of areas demanding further study of a more specialized depth.

title: Plato's Republic
description:
A deconstruction of the major time-worn prejudices and presuppositions of Platonism by way of
a radical reading of Plato's great dialogue the REPUBLIC.

title: Applied Ethics
description: The application of basic ethical theories to contemporary moral issues drawn from such fields as medicine, business and the environment.

title: Ethics
description: A critical study of diverse moral norms, ideals and systems in theory and practice.

title: Logic & Interpretation
description: Theory and practice of valid principles of thinking including developing the skills of justifying diverse types of belief and evaluating reasons for conflicting standpoints (for example, racial, gender, and ethnic differences).

title: Philosophy of Art
description: Examination of the qualities involved in the appreciation of beauty which serve as standards of taste.

title: American Philosophy
description: Great American thinkers, including thinkers such as Emerson (transcendentalism), Peirce, James, Dewey, and Rorty (pragmatism), Royce (idealism), Quine (analytic philosophy), and de Man (post-structuralism).

title: Comparative Philosophy
description: The relations of the world's philosphies to the basic cultural and religious traditions of the world and to the development of the world community.

title: Current Phil Trends
description: Selected reading in contemporary thought embracing such movements as realism, Marxism, post-structuralism, deconstruction, and postmodernism.

title: Philosophy of Sex
description: Introduction to some of the basic authors, texts, and themes in this branch of philosophy beginning with Plato's SYMPOSIUM.

title: Sexual Orient & Gender
description: An introduction to the philosophy of sexual orientation and its relation to gender, with a special focus on issues of knowledge and politics.

title: Philosophy of Science
description: Reflections on crucial concepts of modern science relevant to philosophical issues in interpreting man and the universe; special attention given to epistemological and other problems of mathematics and physical and social sciences.

title: Philosophy of Feminism
description: An introduction to contemporary feminist theory including discussion of current gender-related issues.

title: Humanities Internship
description: This course provides the academic structure for- and assessment of- an internship experience.

title: Ancient Philosophy
description: Advanced study of major philosophers drawn from the ancient Greek and Roman period.

title: Modern Philosophy
description: Advanced study of major movements in philosophy from the 17th century on, movements such as rationalism, empiricism, idealism, and existentialism.

title: Metaphysics
description: Advanced study of the most basic nature of reality.  (PR: 3 hrs. of philosophy).

title: Philosophy of Knowledge
description: Advanced study of the nature and possibility of knowledge.

title: Phil of History & Culture
description: Ancient and modern theories of the meaning and consequence of history and culture.

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Theories of the nature and functions of religion, including the meaning of religious language and the problems of belief.

title: Phil of Politics & Power
description:
Advanced study of the significance or the place in human reality of political organization,
negotiation, strategy and power.

title: Existential Philosophy
description: A study of existential philosophers from Kierkegaard to Heidegger, Sartre and beyond.

title: Philosophy of Logic
description:
Advanced study of the nature of logic: whether logic is possible at all, how far it applies, and 
whether and how there can be conflicting logics.

description:
Designed for majors as a senior humanities seminar and the culminating
interdisciplinary study in the humanities program.  (Same as CL 490 - 494 and RST 490 - 494)

title: Indoor Cycling
description: Designed to promote fitness through spinning.

title: Total Body Conditioning
description: Designed to help instruct both men and women with a variety of lifetime fitness activities.

title: Pickle Ball
description: Learn the fundamentals of pickleball, from rules to techniques, in this engaging PEL course. Develop skills in serving, volleying, and strategy for all levels.

title: Rock Climbing
description: Through activity, the students will learn the fundamentals and techniques of rock climbing.

title: Core Conditioning
description: Designed to help instruct both men and women on numerous activities to help strengthen the abdominal region of the body which can then be incorporated into their workout program.

title: Mindfulness and Yoga
description: Discover the transformative power of yoga and mindfulness. Cultivate inner peace, flexibility, and resilience through movement.

title: Advanced Self Defense
description:
Builds on techniques and strategies from PEL 120 , adds defenses against the 
edged weapon and firearm.  Course covers more prone defense strategies, 
multiple subject encounters and low and diffused light simulation.

title: Intermediate Yoga
description: This course is an intermediate yoga class building off of beginning yoga.  It will focus on poses, breathing, relaxation, and meditation.

title: Introductory Astronomy (CT)
description: A survey of the past, present, and future of the Universe, from our solar system, to the nearby stars, our Milky Way galaxy and far beyond.

title: Introductory Astronomy Lab
description: Laboratory to accompany PS 101 , focuses on the Solar System, stars and their lifecycles, the Milky Way, the origin and evolution of the Universe, the search for life elsewhere, and related topics

title: Life in the Universe
description: An investigation of the prospects for the existence of life elsewhere in our solar system, galaxy, and the wider Universe.  Includes an integrated 2 hour lab.

title: PS for Teachers: Planets
description:
A conceptual study of the Earth's relation to the other members of the solar system and its place in the Universe.  
(Part of a 3 course sequence for K-9 education majors).

title: PS for Teachers: Chemistry
description: PS 121 is a part of a 3 course sequence of Physical Science for K-9 Education majors.  Includes 2-hr, 1 credit lab.

title: PS for Teachers: Physics
description: PS 122 is part of a 3 course sequence of Physical Science for K-9 Education majors.  Includes 2-hr, 1 credit lab.

title: Dev Scientific Thought
description: An introduction to the history and nature of science emphasizing the logic of scientific reasoning and progress with social and historical influences. Includes lab. (PR: 12 hrs of Science)

title: Astronomy
description: A study of the stars, planets, and galaxies, planetary motion, cosmology, cosmography.  Designed to assist teachers and others to develop an interest in astronomy.

title: Astronomy Laboratory
description: A computational and observational laboratory.  Fundamental observations in astronomy and their interpretation through physical laws.  Quantitative discussion of orbital motion, time, telescopes, solar system, stars, and galaxies.

title: Remote Sensing w Applications
description: A study of the physical systems for collecting remotely sensed data.  Statistical/spatial analysis and modeling using image processing/geographic information/spatial analysis computer software systems with earth resource applications. (PR: PHY 203 and PHY 204 )

title: Image Processing/Modeling
description: A study of image processing/geographic information and spatial analysis hardware/software systems, concurrent and parallel image processing modeling scenarios utilizing geobiophysical data for computer simulation modeling and practicum.

title: Practicum
description: Problem solving, geobiophysical modeling, and proposal development techniques in the physical sciences.

title: Conceptual Physics
description: Introduces nonscience majors to applications of physics in life.  Emphasizes conceptual understanding of basic principles in classical and modern physics.  Recommended for science students with no high school physics.

title: Conceptual Physics Lab
description: Conceptual Physics Laboratory. A laboratory course designed to include the principles and applications of physics that are introduced in Physics 101.  (CR: PHY 101 ) 2 lab.

title: Sound & Light in Nature (CT)
description: This course showcases the simple physics found in music, light, force, energy, electricity, magnetism, nature, and the arts including the basic physics of musical instruments.

title: Sound & Light in Nature Lab
description: PHY 102L is a hands-on lab course designed to include the principles and applications of physics that are introduced in PHY 102 .

title: Overview of Physics (CT)
description: An algebra-based overview of well-established topics studied as part of a major in physics, including classical physics, special relativity, quantum mechanics, particle physics, and cosmology.

title: College Physics I
description: First half of an introduction to physics for life-science students, using algebra and trigonometry, including kinematics and dynamics, force, energy, rotation, fluids, waves, and therma phenomena.

title: General Physics I Laboratory
description: Laboratory to accompany PHY 201 or PHY 211 , focusing on mechanics concepts and applications.

title: College Physics II
description: Second half of an introduction to physics for life-science students, using algebra and trigonometry, including electric and magnetic fields, circuits, geometrical and physical optics, atomic and nuclear physics.

title: General Physics 2 Laboratory
description: Laboratory to accompany PHY 203 or PHY 213 , focusing on classical E&M, circuits, and optics.

title: University Physics I
description: First half of an introduction to physics for physical science and engineering students, using calculus, and including kinematics and dynamics, force, energy, rotation, fluids, waves, and thermal phenomena.

title: University Physics II
description: Second half of an introduction to physics for physical science or engineering students, using calculus, including electric and magnetic fields, circuits, geometrical and physical optics, atomic and nuclear physics.

title: Lab Methods in Physics
description: A laboratory course to accompany Physics 211-213.  3 lab.

title: Investigate the Universe
description: A creative laboratory course designed to give students an opporutnity to work with modern research equipment, with ample time to conduct experiments and/or investigate phenomenae of their choosing.

title: Enhancement for Physics I
description: Additional aspects in mechanics (calculus, array vectors and matrices, distributions),  to make College Physics 1 equivalent to University Physics 1.

title: Enhancement for Physics II
description: Additional aspects in E&M and Optics, Modern Physics, (calculus and multipole functions) to make College Physics 2 equivalent to University Physics 2.

title: Electricity & Magnetism
description: A course including the study of electrostatics, magnetostatics, electromagnetic induction, introduction to Maxwell's equations and electromagnetic waves.  3 lec.

title: Electricity & Magnetism II
description: A study of Maxwell's equations and electromagnetic waves, radiation theory, optical phenomena, and electrodynamics. 3 lec.

title: Optics
description: An intermediate course in geometrical and physical optics. 3 lec.

title: Electronic Physics
description: A study of transistors, integrated circuits and their associated circuits.  3 lec.

title: Intro Modern Physics
description: An introductory study of atomic and molecular theories, relativity, quantum theory, and nuclear physics.  3 lec.

title: Mechanics
description: An intermediate study of the fundamental principles of statics of particles and rigid bodies, momentum and energy, dynamics of particles, harmonic oscillations, and wave motion.  3 lec.

title: Scientific Computing
description: Introduction to some of the most important tools and techniques in scientific computing, including object-oriented design, version control, and MPI for high-performance computing.

title: Biological Physics
description:
Physical principles underlying the mechanisms by which living organisms survive, adapt, grow.  Will enhance writing skills and strategies.  2 lec - 2 lab.  
(PR: PHY 203 or 213 , and PHY 204 )

title: Medical Physics
description: Physical principles applied to devise methods for diagnostic and treatment of the human body.  Will enhance writing skills and strategies.

title: Optics Lab
description: A course in optical experiments encompassing geometrical and physical optics. This course is to be taken with Physics 304.

title: Thermal Stat Physics
description: Introduction to Thermodynamics, kinetic theory of gases, classical and quantum statistical mechanics, Bose-Einstein and Fermi-Dirac statistics, and application.  3 lec.

title: Electronics Lab
description: A course in laboratory measurements encompassing transistors, integrated circuits, and their associated circuits. This course is to be taken with Physics 314.

title: Astrophysics
description: A detailed study of core problems in Astrophysics such as orbital dynamics, radiation processes, stellar structure and evolution, galactic dynamics, and cosmology.

title: Modern Physics Lab
description: Laboratory exercises on modern physics topics encompassing both experiments of historic significance and current applications. To be taken with Physics 320, or equivalent.

title: Solid State Physics
description: The course provides a broad introduction to the structure and physical properties of solids. It also serves as a basis for advanced courses in solid state and condensed matter physics.

title: Computational Physics
description: A course on using numerical methods and computer programming languages for solving complex physics problems and for the simulation of various physical processes. 2 lec-2 lab.

title: Quantum Mechanics
description: A study of waves and particles, the Schroedinger and Heisenberg formulations, particles in potential fields, scattering and perturbation theories, and application to atomic and nuclear structure.  3 lec.

title: Quantum Mechanics II
description: This is the second part of a two-semester introduction to quantum mechanics. Emphasis is on applications of quantum theory including approximation techniques and the study of more realistic quantum systems.

title: Advanced Laboratory
description: Developments in producing and detecting correlated photon pairs has enabled implementation of undergraduate laboratories  demonstrating fundamental quantum mechanical principles.  This laboratory also incorporates fundamental solid state and materials science experiments.

title: Math Methods of Physics
description: An introduction to theory of orthogonal functions, curvilinear coordinate systems, vector and tensor fields, and their applications in physics.  Problems are drawn from different areas of physics.  3 lec.

title: Math Methods of Physics II
description: A second semester of a full year course on methods of solving problems in physics: calculus of variations, ordinary and partial differential equations, and special functions with real physics problems.

title: Mechanics for Teachers
description: An indepth study of mechanics for education majors specializing in Physics with emphasis on problem solving techniques, demonstrations, experiments and computer applications. (PR: PHY 203 , MTH 122 and MTH 140 )

title: Capstone
description: To give a capstone expeience to physics majors in their junior or senior years by applying the principles of physics to the solution of real life problems.  (PR: CSD 203, or 218 and lab).

title: American Government (CT)
description: This course will engage students in critical thinking as they explore the American federal government system, with emphasis on constiutionalism, governmental structure, and the political process.

title: Fundamentals of Politics (CT)
description: General survey introducing the study of politics, its major concepts, processes, institutions, and fields of concern, with attention to the place of political science in the larger context of social science inquiry.

title: Models of Politics
description: This course explores theoretical frameworks and models in political science, providing students with foundational tools to analyze, understand, and predict political systems, behaviors, and institutions.

title: Politics, Media, and Culture
description: An introductory survey of how the media and popular culture both reflect and shape political values.

title: State and Local Government
description: Study of the institutions, processes, and significance of this level of political life in America.

title: Comparative Politics (CT)
description: Introduction to the field of comparative politics, stressing comparative concepts and approaches to the cross-national study of politics and government, with examination of political systems, ranging from democratic to non-democratic types.

title: World Politics (CT)
description: Survey of major concepts and approaches in the study of international relations and analysis of processes, institutions, strategies, and trends in world politics.

title: The U.S. Constitution
description: This course explores the U.S. Constitution as a legal framework and political symbol, examining its structure, interpretation, mythologies, and role in shaping American democracy, identity, power, and institutional conflict.

title: Special Topics
description: To offer a course on some special topic not adequately treated in the regular course offerings.

title: Special Topics
description: To offer a course on some special topic not adequately treated in the regular course offerings.

title: Special Topics
description: To offer a course on some special topic not adequately treated in the regular course offerings.

title: Special Topics
description: To offer a course on some special topic not adequately treated in the regular course offerings.

title: City Management
description: Political systems in American cities and metropolitan areas.

title: Models of Public Policy
description: Basic concepts and skills in the analysis of public policy problems.  Use of policy as an instrument for solving problems.  Application to selected fields, for example, environmental policy and urban policy.

title: American Political Parties
description: Examination of the American party system, its origins, development and characteristics.  Emphasis also on party organization, political ambition and recruitment, party impact on public policy, campaigns, elections, and voting behavior.

title: Law & Society
description: Explores the relationship between legal systems and society, examining how laws shape and are shaped by social structures, institutions, norms, and issues of justice, and policy, through interdisciplinary analysis.

title: Public Opinion and Propaganda
description: Study of the processes by which individuals acquire politically relevant information, attitudes, values, and opinions; the consequences of these processes for political stability and conflict; and the linkage of mass opinions to elite behavior.  (Same as Sociology 307)

title: Campaigns and Elections
description: Federal and state laws for campaigns and elections and the changing mechanisms for reaching voters are covered in this course.

title: Issues in Public Policy
description: A course devoted to a special topic of interest in the policy field, such as energy, health care, transportation, environmental concerns, etc.

title: Research in Political Science
description: Study of the development of political science as a distinct science and discipline, and of the fundamentals of research in political science, such as bibliographic techniques, uses of scientific method, textual and case-study approaches and data analysis.

title: Public Administration
description: Introduction to modern theories of administration; the relation of administration to the political system and process; and analysis of administrative organizations and functions, including planning, personnel, and finance.

title: Race & Politics
description: Study emphasizing power structures in black communities, dissent and protest, problems and trends, and the uniqueness of black politics as compared with the politics of other ethnic groups.

title: The US Congress
description: Structure and behavior of American national and state legislative systems; the impact of constituencies, parties, interest groups, interpersonal relations, and other factors on the legislative policymaking process; the role of the legislature as a subsystem in the larger political system; and problems and trends.

title: Student Legislative Program
description: One week of intensive legislative observation designed to provide selected students an understanding of the organization and processes of the West Virginia legislature and its role in the making of public policy.

title: The American Presidency
description: Study of governmental executives in the American political system, with emphasis on the president, including analysis of constitutional status and powers, recruitment, administrative responsibilities, political and legislative leadership, accountability, and problems and trends.

title: Politics of the Undead
description: An exploration of political thought, international relations theory, and political economy through the pop cultural lens of vampires, zombies, and other undead creatures.

title: War and Pop Culture
description: An exploration of evolving depictions of political violence in film, television, graphic novels, video games, and popular music.

title: UN & Global Organizations
description: Study of world and regional organizations as reflections of world politics, as instruments of foreign policies, and as forces for change and order, with emphasis on their role as channels for management of cooperation and conflict.

title: International Relations
description: Study of major issues in world politics, with emphasis on theoretical approaches, problems of war and peace, and contemporary trends.

title: Western Democratic Politics
description: Study of such nations as Canada and those of Western Europe, particularly Great Britian and France.

title: Russian Politics
description: Study of the politics of Russia and the former Soviet Union.

title: Latin American Politics
description: Study of Latin American politics by sectors, such as landed elites, the military, the church, etc.  Various styles of governance are considered.  Case examples illustrate concepts discussed.

title: International Political Econ
description: This course will examine the evolution and structure of the global economic system with emphasis on the development of the Liberal International Economic Order.

title: International Law
description: Study of theories, origins, sources, development, present state, and trends of international law as a factor in various aspects of international politics.

title: Politics of Development
description: A survey of major theories of development and modernization and issues confronting developing nations around the world.

title: American Political Thought II
description: This course is a detailed examination of the philosophical and historical roots of American politics from Reconstruction through the present with emphasis on original texts.

title: Women and Political Thought
description: This course examines how women were conceptualized in the history of political philosophy and how women then began conceptualizing themselves and their relation to politics.

title: Issues in World Politics
description: An intensive study of specific world or regional problems, such as the politics of world hunger, the role of multinational corporations, imperialism, Third World Communist movements, etc.

title: American Political Thought I
description: This course is a detailed examination of the philosophical and historical roots of American politics from the Colonial era through the Civil War with emphasis on original texts.

title: American Foreign Policy
description: The study of descriptive, analytical, and normative aspects of United States foreign policy with emphasis on contemporary problems and issues.

title: Diplomacy
description: Application of the comparative method to foreign policy decision-making and outputs.  Comparisons within or between geographic regions.

title: Classical Political Thought
description: Selective study of classics of Western political theory from earliest times through the 15th century, such as that of Plato, Aristotle, the Romans, Augustine, and Aquinas.

title: Modern Political Thought
description: Selective study of classics of Western political theory from the 16th century through the 19th century, such as that of Machiavelli, Bodin, Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau, Hume, Burke, Mill, and Marx.

title: Shapers and Definers
description:
A study of political leaders who have shaped and defined the American constitutional 
tradition.

title: Conflict and Revolution
description: Study of major theories of conflict and revolution with emphasis on cross-national explanations and outcomes.

title: Political Ideologies
description: This course examines modern political ideologies including Liberalism, Conservatism, Anarchism, Socialism, Facism, Feminism, and Environmentalism with emphasis on the original texts.

title: Global Terrorism
description: An examination of terrorism globally, both in its development and its current manifestations, with attention to its attractions, the difficulties of confronting it, and its implications for democratic society.

title: Nonprofit Management
description: This course examines the principles and applied practices of nonprofit administration including theories of nonprofit formation, fundraising appeals, executive leadership, marketing, budgeting, and strategic management.

title: Public Policy Development
description: Examination of alternative theoretical approaches to the study of policy and administration and their implications for the use of policy to shape administrative practice.

title: Intel and Covert Ops
description: An introduction to the institutions and processes of the U.S. Intelligence Community, with an emphasis on the vital role of intelligence in national security.

title: Harry Potter Political Theory
description: Detailed examination of the Harry Potter book series through the lens of various theories and theorists of power, with emphasis on scholarly argumentation and writing.

title: US Court System
description: Structure and behavior in American national and state judicial systems, including analysis of their decision making and policy making functions, their procedures and administration, and problems and trends.

title: Power in American Society
description: Examination of some of the major theoretical approaches pluralistic, elitist, etc. to the study of power.  A major concern is the relationship between the distribution of political resources and the performance of political systems.  Efforts to transform political systems are examined on the basis of cross-national research.

title: Politics of Poverty
description: A comparative course examining the political institutional methods states use to assist citizens who are poor, primarily women and children. It also addresses behavioral concerns that shape welfare policy.

title: Dictatorship & Democracy
description:
An investigation of the strengths of democracies relative to dictatorships with regard to such
dimensions as economic growth, income equality, health and welfare of citizens and 
war reductions.

title: Environmental Politics
description: This course examines multiple prespectives on the relationship between humans and nature, focusing on how particular interpretations of this relationship determine how we translate environmental concerns into political problems.

title: Politics in History
description:
A study of politics as an order shattering, order restructuring force during some of America's
most transformative moments.

title: Administrative Law
description: A study of the basic legal framework of administrative organization, including the problems of administrative discretion, rule-making and adjudication, regulatory agencies, and administrative responsibility in the democratic state.

title: Public Personnel
description: Survey of Public Personnel Administration with particular attention to various facets of the merit system concept. Psychological and human relations aspects of the work situation and supervisor-subordinate interaction emphasized.

title: Public Finance Management
description: Study of organization, administration, and accountability in the management of public funds, with emphasis on the political decision-making processes of budget formulation, presentation and execution.

title: Public Organizations
description: A study of the contributions of the behavioral sciences to the study of organizations with stress on such concepts as leadership, motivation, power conflict, organizational design and decision making.

title: Civil Rights & Liberties
description: The basic substantive and procedural elements of American constitutional liberties and civil rights with emphasis on historical development as influenced by social and political forces.

title: American Cities
description: Study of policy problems of metropolitan areas in terms of structures, alternatives, and outcomes.

title: Appalachian Politics
description: Explores Appalachia as both a geographical region and a political construct, focusing on how politics shapes regional identity and the region's relationship to the United States.

title: Politics and Video Games
description: An exploration of the power dynamics and politics at play in video games, the video game industry, and gaming culture.
